entry.title: Re: Need Centralized Maintenance of Date Input Controls A...
entry.content:
Hi @Suraj34 ,
SAP doesn't provide a single "update once, apply everywhere" mechanism for input controls across separate stories but in most cases like this, the better fix is to remove the need for manual maintenance entirely rather than centralize it.
1. Switch to Dynamic Date Range Filters
If the date selection is something like "last month" or "current quarter," use a Dynamic Date Range Filter instead of a static date pick. These recalculate automatically based on the system date, so nobody has to reset them on the 1st of the month. This alone usually eliminates the missed-report problem, since there's nothing left to forget.
2. Custom Current Date (CCD) if you need a fixed reference date, not "today"
If your use case genuinely needs a specific reference date (not just "today"), each story supports one Custom Current Date input control, which all dynamic date range filters within that story can link to. Set the CCD once and every linked filter in that story recalculates from it. Note this is per-story it doesn't sync across your other 19 reports.
3. True cross-report propagation of one date value
There's no native SAP feature for this. If you need it anyway, the practical options are:

  • Launch all reports from a central point (Digital Boardroom, collection, or launchpad) and pass the date via URL parameters (mParams) so it's set on open requires a shared entry point rather than opening each story individually.
  • If the reports share a model, drive the date restriction from a model-level variable rather than a story filter, so updating it once at the model updates every story built on that model.
  • In Optimized Story Experience, a script object using Application.setVariableValue() can set it programmatically, but only within the story it runs in it doesn't broadcast to other stories.

Bottom line: start with #1. It's very likely the actual root cause of your "reports get missed" problem, and it removes the maintenance task rather than centralizing it.

entry.title: Request for Clarification: HTTP Adapter Retry Configuration
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

SAP previously informed us that the retry functionality can only be applied at the adapter level and cannot be enabled individually for specific programs.

After applying the patch, when we add a new HTTP Adapter within the current IS program, a Retry tab is now available.

Currently, the Retry on Exception and Retry on Error Response options are disabled.

Could you please confirm whether enabling these options would allow the retry functionality to be applied only to the specific program, or whether it would affect all HTTP Adapters across the system?

 

 

[05/July/2026 Monthly Patch description]

Cloud Integration－HTTP Receiver Adapter Retry Configuration Enhancements Available Now

You can now access and manage enhanced retry configurations for the HTTP Receiver Adapter through the Retry tab.

  • Retry on Exception and its related options are now available in the Retry tab.

  • Retry Failed Request f

entry.title: Re: Displaying Actual PR Final Approval Date in ME5A Report
entry.content:
It is not possible with standard SAP to directly show the workflow approval date in ME5A. You will need either:

  • a custom field + enhancement to store the date at PR level, or

  • a custom report that reads workflow tables and displays the approval date.

entry.title: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot designed to enhance user productivity across SAP applications. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, Joule empowers users to discover applications, access contextual business insights, and navigate business processes conversationally directly within the SAP Fiori experience.

This document provides a comprehensive, end-to-end technical guide for setting up SAP Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. It is written from an administrator and platform engineering perspective, explaining not only how to configure each component, but also why it is required and how the overall integration functions.

Architecture Overview

 

At a high level, Joule runs on SAP BTP and is embedded into the SAP user experience using SAP Build Work Zone. Authentication and authorization are handled centrally through SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Prerequisites and Readiness Checklist

Before proceeding with the setup, ensure the following prerequisites are in place:

Authorizations

  • SAP BTP Global Account Administrator
  • SAP BTP Subaccount Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS & IPS) Administrator
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Administrator

Entitlements

  • SAP Joule (Digital Assistant Service – das-application)
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Landscape Requirements

  • Supported for both 2-System Landscape (2SL) and 3-System Landscape (3SL)
  • SAP Cloud Connector is not required for Public Cloud systems

Technical Preconditions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant registered in BTP System Landscape
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant available and accessible
  • Browser allows iFrames and third-party cookies

Preparation

SAP BTP Subaccount Strategy

A dedicated SAP BTP subaccount is typically used to host Joule and SAP Build Work Zone. From a design perspective, regional alignment with SAP Cloud Identity Services is recommended to minimize authentication latency and reduce cross-region dependencies.

The subaccount acts as the orchestration layer where:

  • Joule services are provisioned
  • Work Zone acts as the user entry point
  • Trust and identity integration converge

Identity Trust and Domain Alignment
Single Sign-On between SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, and SAP Build Work Zone is achieved through trust establishment with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

A critical design consideration here is domain alignment:

  • The IAS domain selected must align with the domain model of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant
  • Mismatched domains are a frequent root cause of repeated authentication prompts and intermittent Joule access issues

Trusted domain configuration on both BTP and IAS is essential for enabling secure cross-origin communication and embedded user experiences.

Identity Attribute Readiness
Joule and SAP Build Work Zone rely on a consistent identity model provided by IAS. Certain core user attributes must be available and consumable by downstream applications.

From an operational perspective:

  • Attribute completeness matters more than attribute volume
  • Missing verification-related attributes can result in silent authentication failures that are difficult to trace at runtime

Ensuring identity consistency early on significantly reduces troubleshooting effort later.

Joule Enablement Model
Guided Onboarding via Booster

SAP provides a Joule Booster that automates the majority of the technical enablement steps. This guided onboarding mechanism establishes the baseline configuration required for Joule to operate within the BTP subaccount.

Conceptually, the booster:

  • Validates entitlements and platform readiness
  • Establishes system formations
  • Creates the necessary connectivity artifacts
  • Activates SAP-delivered communication scenarios

 

Architecturally, this step should be treated as a managed onboarding process rather than a manual configuration exercise.

SAP Build Work Zone Integration

SAP Build Work Zone serves as the experience layer through which Joule is surfaced to end users. Key design elements include:

  • Subscription to the Work Zone service
  • Secure consumption of S/4HANA Cloud content
  • Centralized role-based navigation and access control.

Work Zone consumes S/4HANA Cloud content using SAP-delivered exposure mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized applications, catalogs, and groups are visible.

Content Exposure Model
Content exposure from SAP S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Build Work Zone is governed by standard communication scenarios delivered by SAP. These scenarios enable controlled sharing of Fiori content without custom integration.

From a best-practice perspective:

  • Content exposure should be validated, not modified
  • Role and catalog assignments remain the authoritative control mechanism

This approach preserves clean-core principles while enabling rich user experiences.

Identity Provisioning Architecture
Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) are used to synchronize users and role assignments into SAP Build Work Zone.

Rather than being treated as a one-time activity, identity provisioning should be viewed as:

  • A continuous synchronization mechanism
  • A dependency for consistent authorization behavior
  • A key operational component for Joule visibility

Operational validation of IPS jobs and logs is a critical checkpoint during the initial enablement.

Validation and Operational Readiness
Once the platform setup is complete, validation should focus on the end-to-end user experience rather than on individual configuration items.

Typical validation checkpoints include:

  • Successful single sign-on from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Visibility of SAP Build Work Zone content
  • Availability of the Joule interaction entry point
  • Execution of basic conversational queries

A successful validation confirms that Joule is correctly embedded and operational.

 

Common Operational Observations
Based on real-world implementations, the following patterns are frequently observed:

  • Repeated login prompts usually indicate trust or domain misalignment
  • Missing Joule entry points are commonly linked to role assignment or identity synchronization gaps
  • Empty or incomplete content typically points to content exposure or IPS issues

Systematically addressing these areas resolves the majority of post-enablement issues.

entry.title: Re: Sap fico
entry.content:
Hi @Aditya_-004,

Great to see your interest in Financial Accounting. If you're just getting started, I recommend beginning with the SAP Learning site, Financial Accounting there you can learn how to process, analyze, and automate intelligent accounting solutions. You find structured, role-based learning journeys designed specifically for beginners and aspiring consultants. SAP offers guided learning paths, hands-on practice opportunities, and certifications that help you build both conceptual knowledge and practical skills. 

A good learning sequence would be:

  • Understand the fundamentals of Financial Accounting (FI),e.g. have a look at Outlining the Financial Accounting Overview 
    This training will help SAP Finance newcomers effectively onboard in the record to report area.
  • Explore SAP S/4HANA Finance business processes and integration scenarios
  • Complete hands-on exercises in SAP systems whenever possible
  • Consider pursuing an SAP certification, e.g. SAP CERTIFICATION • C_TS4FI SAP Certified - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Financial Accounting  once you have built a solid foundation

Wishing you success on your SAP learning journey—stay curious, stay persistent, and enjoy the process of turning knowledge into expertise! 

Kind regards
Margit

1) Yes, the standard training systems and practical exercises from our courses are absolutely the best way to prepare. I am 100% sure that if you go through the exercises and understand the steps, you will have no problem to pass the exam.

entry.title: Re: Reprocessing of Retried Messages in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Hi Venkat,

Please find below the available options for handling failed messages in SAP CPI based on the two scenarios:

Scenario 1: Sender System Messages Not Delivered to CPI

In this case, the message never reached CPI, so the retry must be handled on the sender side:

  • SFTP — The file remains in the source folder and will be re-polled on the next scheduled run
  • IDoc — Retry can be triggered from the SAP backend via SM58 / BD87
  • HTTP/SOAP — The calling system must resend the request
  • AS2 — The sender must resend after receiving a negative MDN from CPI

Note: These messages will not appear in the CPI monitor as they never entered the system.

Scenario 2: Messages Reached CPI but Failed During Processing

For messages that entered CPI but failed within the integration flow, the following options are available:

  • Monitor → Message Processing — View error details and manually retry the failed message
  • JMS Dead Letter Queue — Reprocess messages from the DLQ via Monitor → Manage Stores → JMS Resources
  • Data Store — Retrieve stored message payload and reprocess manually
  • Automatic Retry — Retry count and intervals can be configured within the adapter settings
  • Alert Rules — Alerts can be set up to notify the team via email upon failure

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning
entry.content:
Hello, SAP Community members! 

If you have worked with the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise, you already know how powerful it is. The ability to split a large dataset into ranges and run your processing functions package by package is, in many models, the difference between a job that completes in minutes and one that ties up your system for hours. The idea is elegant: define your ranges once at design time, and the engine does the rest. 

For SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model customers, this raises a natural question: does the Cloud Partitioning give me the same control I had On-Premise, including the ability to determine ranges dynamically at runtime rather than locking them in at design time? With the introduction of Dynamic Packaging Type, the answer is yes. 

The Dynamic Packaging Type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model follows the same principle as its On-Premise counterpart: ranges are no longer pre-defined as inner nodes but are determined at runtime from the input data of the function where partitioning is applied. For background on the On-Premise partitioning and packaging setup, see the SAP Help Portal documentation.

Meet the Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning

The Dynamic Packaging Type is found in the Partitioning within the General Entities. This is a new packaging option that brings a fundamentally different approach to how processing operations are split across data ranges at runtime. Until now, only Static packaging was available in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model; you define your ranges as inner nodes at design time, and those ranges are fixed for every run. 

One key structural difference worth knowing upfront: with Dynamic Packaging, you no longer define range nodes inside the Partitioning at all. Any range nodes previously defined inside a Static Packaging are removed when you switch to Dynamic. The ranges are built fresh at runtime, derived from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied.

Configuring the Partitioning: Packaging Type

Expand the Partitioning in your model. The Properties panel now includes the new field placed directly after Run Mode. 

Packaging Type: Determining How Ranges Are Defined 

This is the central new field introduced by this enhancement. 

Packaging Type (dropdown, mandatory): Determines whether ranges are defined at design time or derived at runtime. 

The dropdown offers two values: 

  • Static 
  • Dynamic 

Static preserves the existing behavior — ranges are defined as inner nodes inside the Partitioning at design time, exactly as before. 

Dynamic is the new Packaging Type introduced by this enhancement. Unlike Static, where ranges are locked in at design time, Dynamic determines partition ranges at runtime — derived automatically from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied. Note that switching an existing Partitioning from Static to Dynamic removes any range nodes previously defined inside it. 

The default value for Packaging Type is Static. Existing Partitioning built without this field are treated as Static automatically. No migration of existing configurations is required. 

Packaging Field: Defining the Field for Dynamic Range Creation 

Another mandatory companion to Packaging Type, present for both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Packaging Field (dropdown, mandatory): Defines which field values are used to create the partition ranges at runtime.

Note: The field previously labeled Field in the Partitioning has been renamed to Packaging Field. This rename applies to all Partitioning, both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Before You Begin

Before configuring a Dynamic Packaging Type, keep the following constraints in mind: 

  • Range Levels and Destinations are not supported for Dynamic Packaging Type. These features are only available with Static packaging. 
  • For Dynamic Packaging Type, the following Run Mode values are not displayed in the dropdown when Dynamic is selected: PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, SUXP. 
  • For a given Packaging Field, you can define multiple Partitioning as long as each has a different Run Mode or Packaging Type setting.

How It Works

To help illustrate, here is a complete end-to-end walkthrough of configuring and running a Partitioning with Dynamic Packaging Type. 

  1. Open your model in the Modeling environment and locate the Partitioning you want to configure (or create a new one from the Palette). 
  2. Open the Properties panel. Locate the Packaging Type field, placed directly after Run Mode. 
  3. Set Packaging Type to Dynamic.  
  4. Set Packaging Field to the field whose values will drive range creation at runtime. Confirm that this field exists as an input to the function where dynamic partitioning will be applied. 
    Note that PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP Run Mode values will not be displayed in the Run Mode dropdown when Dynamic Packaging Type is selected.
  5. Save the Partitioning configuration. 
  6. Execute your process. At runtime, the system reads the distinct values of the Packaging Field from the input function entries and constructs the partition ranges dynamically before processing begins. 
  7. Check the Application Monitor to confirm the run completed and to see how many package instances were created. In our example, if the input function contains entries for four customers, the engine creates four partition ranges at runtime and processes each as a separate package instance. 

This is the complete end-to-end flow: the Packaging Type field drives whether ranges come from design-time nodes or runtime data, the Packaging Field identifies the dimension that structures those runtime ranges, and the engine handles range creation automatically before execution begins. See SAP Help related to Partitioning and Range Setup for further details. 

Wrapping Up

The Dynamic Packaging Type removes a configuration step that, for many models, was also a maintenance burden: keeping design-time range nodes synchronized with the actual data in your system. Whether you work with a cost center hierarchy that changes frequently and you want packaging to always reflect the current structure, a profit center landscape that varies by version and requires a different set of ranges for each scenario, or a model where the right partition boundaries simply cannot be known until the data arrives at runtime, Dynamic Packaging Type gives you a clean, supported way to let the data define its own partitions. 

We are eager to hear from you. Which datasets in your models are you thinking of migrating from Static to Dynamic packaging? Have you encountered situations where design-time range maintenance was a limiting factor in your partitioning strategy? Share your thoughts in the comments below and stay tuned for further enhancements to the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. 

As always, feel free to reach out via the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Community if you have questions. 

Related Resources 

  • Partitioning and Range Setup - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Different Run Modes - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Partitioning and Packaging Setup - SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise

entry.title: Building Vector Search Applications with SAP HANA Cloud and LangChain.js
entry.content:
Imagine your customer types “something comfortable for working from home” into your shop’s search bar. There’s no “comfortable” column in your product table. No “work from home” tag either. And yet your store somehow needs to surface the ergonomic chair, the noise-cancelling headphones, and the standing desk.

That gap between what people say and what your database stores is exactly where vector search lives. With @SAP/hana-langchain, your SAP HANA Cloud database doesn’t just store your data. It understands it.

The full walkthrough, with every line of code, every output, and every config option, lives on GitHub: blogs/blog-post.md. The sections below focus on the ideas worth remembering, paired with the bits of code and output that bring them to life.

What it is

@SAP/hana-langchain is the official open-source bridge between SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine and LangChain.js. It turns your enterprise database into a first-class AI retrieval layer for RAG, semantic search, recommendations, and intelligent Q&A, all running where your data already lives.

Two lines and you have a vector store:

npm install @langchain/core @langchain/classic langchain
npm install @sap/hana-langchain

Searching by intent, not by keywords

Let’s walk through the real story. A customer is setting up their home office. They open your store and search:

“comfortable work from home setup”

Your catalogue has hundreds of products. Keyword search? Zero relevant hits. With HanaDB? Watch what happens.

import { HanaDB, HanaInternalEmbeddings } from "@sap/hana-langchain";

// Embeddings happen INSIDE HANA. No external API. No data leaving your DB.
const embeddings = new HanaInternalEmbeddings({
  internalEmbeddingModelId: "SAP_NEB.20240715",
});

const store = new HanaDB(embeddings, {
  connection: client,
  tableName: "PRODUCT_CATALOG",
});
await store.initialize();

const results = await store.similaritySearch(
  "comfortable work from home setup",
  2,
);

The output:

[
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Ergonomic office chair with lumbar support and adjustable armrests',
    metadata: { category: 'furniture', price: 449, in_stock: true }
  },
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Professional espresso machine with built-in grinder',
    metadata: { category: 'appliances', price: 899.99, in_stock: false }
  }
]

The chair makes obvious sense. The espresso machine? Also surprisingly on-brand for “working from home.” The model picked up on intent that no SQL LIKE ever could.

Cosine vs. Euclidean: pick your distance

So how does similaritySearch actually decide which products are “closest” to the query? Each document and the query are turned into vectors, and then HanaDB applies a distance metric to measure how far apart they are. The library supports two: cosine (the default) and Euclidean. You pick which one you want with a single config option when you create the store.

The choice matters because the two metrics score the same set of candidates differently. Take the query “high quality audio headphones” and run it under both strategies. Same data, same embedding model, different scoring philosophy:

COSINE Similarity Results (higher = more similar)
[0.7219] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.5506] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.5206] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

EUCLIDEAN Distance Results (lower = more similar)
[0.7086] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.9048] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.9251] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

Both metrics pick the same winner here, but notice the gap between first and second place: cosine spreads the candidates by relative similarity, Euclidean by raw distance in vector space. As a rule of thumb, cosine is the right default for text, because it only cares about the direction the vectors point in, not how long they are. Euclidean is a better fit when the actual position of the vectors carries meaning, for example when your embeddings come from numerical features rather than text. If you’re not sure which to pick, leave it on cosine.

Adding diversity when you need it: MMR

Sometimes “most similar” is exactly what the user wants. A shopper who searches for “wireless headphones” expects a list of wireless headphones, and similarity search delivers that perfectly well.

But there are spots in the same product journey where pure similarity hurts the experience. The classic case is the “customers also viewed” strip on a product detail page. A shopper looking at a premium open-back studio headphone doesn’t need to see four more premium over-ear headphones below it. They’d be much better served by a strip that also shows them a different form factor, a different price tier, or a noise-cancelling alternative.

That’s the job of Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR). Instead of returning the top-k most similar documents, MMR picks results that are both relevant and different from each other. You retrieve a larger candidate pool; MMR walks it, picking the next item that maximises relevance while penalising redundancy with what’s already been chosen. One lambda knob tunes the balance between relevance and diversity.

Same vector store, same query, two different methods. First, plain similarity search:

// Plain similarity search: top-4 candidates ordered by closeness alone
const similar = await vectorStore.similaritySearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  4,
);
similaritySearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  3. AudioMax Buds 5    (true-wireless,     $299.99)
  4. QuietShield Ultra  (over-ear-wireless, $429.99)

Three of the four results are premium over-ear wireless headphones, almost interchangeable for the shopper. Not a useful “customers also viewed” strip.

Now the same query, through MMR:

// MMR: pick 4 from a wider candidate pool while penalising redundancy.
// lambda = 0.5 balances relevance and diversity evenly.
const recommendations = await vectorStore.maxMarginalRelevanceSearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  { k: 4, fetchK: 12, lambda: 0.5 },
);
maxMarginalRelevanceSearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. FlexFit Sport      (neckband,           $89.99)
  3. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  4. TuneCore 510       (on-ear-wireless,    $49.99)

The top match is still the most relevant premium over-ear pair, but the next three deliberately span different form factors and price tiers. The shopper now sees an actual mix to compare, all from one method swap on the same vector store and the same product catalogue.

Use similaritySearch when the goal is the closest match. Reach for maxMarginalRelevanceSearch when the goal is coverage: recommendation strips, RAG context that spans multiple sub-topics, related-articles modules, or anywhere a single dominant theme would crowd out everything else.

Filtering that speaks JavaScript

Semantic search is great. Semantic search plus structured filters is unbeatable. HanaDB’s filter syntax will feel instantly familiar to anyone who’s ever written a MongoDB query:

// In-stock electronics under $500
const filter = {
  $and: [
    { in_stock: true },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price: { $lt: 500 } },
  ],
};

const results = await store.similaritySearch("best audio quality", 10, filter);

And the result is exactly what the filter promised: only in-stock electronics, all under $500, ranked by audio quality:

[
  { pageContent: 'Premium wireless headphones with spatial audio...',  price: 349.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Budget wireless earbuds with decent sound...',       price:  49.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Bluetooth over-ear headphones with active noise...', price: 249.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Wireless noise-canceling headphones with 30-hour...',price: 299.99, in_stock: true }
]

You get the full operator suite ($lt, $lte, $gte, $between, $in, $nin, $like, $contains, $and, $or) all compiled down to native HANA SQL. No JavaScript-side filtering. No surprises at scale.

Letting the LLM write its own filters

Here’s where it gets fun. What if a user types “Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings”? You could build a custom intent parser. Or you could let the LLM do it for free, with the HanaTranslator:

const retriever = SelfQueryRetriever.fromLLM({
  llm,
  vectorStore: store,
  documentContents: "Product description for an e-commerce catalog",
  attributeInfo,                              // describe your metadata
  structuredQueryTranslator: new HanaTranslator(),
});

await retriever.invoke("Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings");

Behind the scenes, the LLM emits:

{
  $and: [
    { brand: "AudioMax" },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price:  { $lt:  400 } },
    { rating: { $gte: 4 } }
  ]
}

Natural language in. Structured filter out. Vector search on top. Zero glue code.

There’s more in the box

What you’ve seen so far is the foundation. The library ships with three more capabilities, each big enough to deserve its own deep-dive:

  • Cross-encoding reranking with HanaReranker. Re-score your top candidates for surgical-precision results, the secret sauce behind great RAG and customer-facing search.
  • Knowledge graphs & SPARQL with HanaRdfGraph. Query RDF data, build natural-language Q&A over relationships, and combine graph reasoning with vector search.
  • Performance at scale with Map Merge, HNSW indexes, and specific metadata columns. Roughly 9× faster bulk inserts, sub-second search at million-document scale, and faster filtered queries.

Stay tuned for more updates and deep dives into each of these capabilities!

Get the code: SAP/langchainjs-integration-for-sap-hana-cloud  |  Read the full guide: blog-post.md

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 Integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working on integrating SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM) and would appreciate your guidance on the recommended integration approach.

I have the following questions:

Q1. Is SAP CPI mandatory for integrating SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise with SAP SFM?

Q2. Is the sap-sfm-integration-accelerator package mandatory for integrating SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise with SAP SFM, or is it optional?

Q3. The SAP SFM 'Manage Source Systems' app lists SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition as a supported source type for CDS-based replication. Does this same replication and extension framework (Manage Replications + Configure Extensions for Replications) also apply to On-Premise 2023, or does On-Premise require a different integration path?

Q4. How can transactional data be imported into SAP SFM? If I want to route a standard CDS view to a custom CDS view during the replication process, where can this mapping or redirection be configured?

Q5. What is the BADI for implementing customer-specific rules for MD and TD in SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise? In SAP SFM, the "Configure Extensions for Replications" app only exposes a CDS View option for extensibility, and no BADI option is available. For scenarios where a CDS view is not sufficient for MD/TD, is there a supported BADI or another enhancement framework for implementing customer-specific MD and TD logic in this integration with SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise? If so, where is it exposed and how can it be implemented?

Thank you for taking the time to read our questions. We greatly appreciate any guidance, suggestions, or experiences you can share.

Regards,
Shiva

entry.title: Re: Inbound Delivery Exceeds PO Quantity Error in SAP S/4...
entry.content:
Dear @laxmiprasad_rkhasnis,

The error M7 022 – PU Ordered quantity exceeded is caused by the quantity in the inbound delivery / goods receipt process exceeding the quantity still allowed for the referenced purchase order item

I reproduced the scenario in Public Cloud as follows:

  • PO quantity: 5 PC

  • Inbound Delivery quantity changed to 6 PC and Putaway to 6 PC

 

  • During the inbound delivery change, the system displayed ME160 Notified quantity exceeds quantity still to be notified 5 PC
  • The inbound delivery could still be saved
  • During stock placement / putaway and posting of goods receipt the system stopped the process with:

M7 022 – PU Ordered quantity exceeded by 1 PC

This confirms that the inbound delivery can be saved with a higher quantity but the system blocks the final goods movement / GR posting when the quantity exceeds the allowed PO quantity

The behavior is standard. During goods receipt the system checks the receipt quantity against the PO quantity and the allowed overdelivery tolerance. If the delivered quantity exceeds the allowed tolerance, the system issues an error

To resolve the issue there are three possible options:

1. Reduce the inbound delivery / putaway / GR quantity
Use this option if the business should only receive the quantity ordered on the PO. In this case the quantity should be reduced to the remaining open PO quantity

2. Increase the PO quantity
Use this option if the ordered quantity itself is incorrect and the PO should really be changed from, for example, 5 PC to 6 PC

3. Maintain overdelivery tolerance on the PO item
Use this option if the PO quantity should remain unchanged but the business allows receipt of a higher quantity

In the PO item in the Delivery section/tab, maintain either:

  • Overdelivery Tolerance Limit, for example 20% if PO quantity is 5 PC and receipt quantity is 6 PC or
  • Unlimited Overdelivery Allowed, if the business wants to allow overdelivery without a percentage limit

For the reproduced example, PO = 5 PC and GR = 6 PC the overdelivery is 1 PC, which equals 20%. Therefore setting Overdelivery Tolerance Limit = 20.0 allowed the GR to be posted successfully. The Underdelivery Tolerance Limit is not relevant for this error because it controls receiving less than the ordered quantity, not more

If the process is warehouse-managed and the error remains in the warehouse queue the corrected document should be reprocessed from the relevant warehouse processing / message queue after the PO quantity or overdelivery tolerance has been corrected

More details below:

SAP Learning – Applying Tolerances and the Delivery Complete Indicator

SAP KBA 3538235 – Error Message M7 022 at stuck queue for inbound deliveries – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: S/4 HANA BRF+: In Sales Order system is triggering th...
entry.content:
Why it defaults to Print is because

Channel determination precedence
In BRF+, if multiple channel rules are valid, the system often picks the first matching one. If your Printer Setting rule (with blank role) is evaluated before the Email Setting rule, the system will assign Print.

Role mismatch
You have defined Email settings for Role = ST (Ship‑to). If the change scenario does not pass Role = ST correctly (e.g., Ship‑to not determined or missing in the change context), the Email rule won’t match, and the fallback “Printer” rule is applied.

Exclusive indicator
Since you set Exclusive = False, multiple rules can be valid. That increases the chance that the Print rule is picked first.

Form Template condition
You have tied the form template to Channel = Email. If the system does not find a matching form template for the change case, it reverts to the default Print channel.


So the next step is

  • Run a BRF+ trace for the VA02 change scenario. This will show exactly which rules are being evaluated and why the Print channel wins. From there, you can:
    • Tighten the conditions on the Printer rule,
    • Or make the Email rule exclusive and prioritized.

entry.title: Academic Resources for Teaching End-to-End Business Processes
entry.content:
Academic Resources for Teaching End-to-End Business Processes

Dear Lecturers,

Join us on July 28 2026 from 10:00–11:00 AM ET | 16:00–17:00 CEST for a live show-and-tell session showcasing three free resources designed to support teaching end-to-end business processes with SAP S/4HANA Cloud. 

  • Online Course – A free 32-hour course available on SAP Learning Hub, student edition
  • Practice System – Access to a preconfigured live SAP S/4HANA Cloud environment
  • Academic Lessons – Ready-to-use teaching materials for the classroom

Got a question? Submit it before the session!

Have something specific you'd like us to address? Comment on this forum post - we'll use your input to tailor the session to your interests.

Interested? Learn more and register here:  Webinar End-to-End Business Processes

We look forward to welcoming you!

Best regards,
SAP University Alliances Program

 

 

Not yet a member of the SAP Community? Here is how to get started.

To leave a comment, you need to be a registered member of the SAP Community with an SAP Universal ID. You may already have one if you have used SAP Learning Hub, student edition before. Click on the avatar in the top right corner to check if you can log in. 

If you do not have an SAP Universal ID yet:

  1. Start the registration process at SAP Universal ID registration.
  2. Add a username in the "Username" field. This name will be displayed in the SAP Community. If you leave it empty, a name will be filled in automatically.
  3. Check your email for an activation link and complete the registration.

If you already have an SAP Universal ID:

  1. Sign in to the SAP Universal ID account manager.
  2. Select Edit.
  3. Enter or update your display name in the "Username" field and click Save.

Important: Please use your real name as your username. Avoid using your email address, company or university name, or random characters.

Once you have your SAP Universal ID, sign in to the SAP Community:

  1. Go to the SAP Community.
  2. Click the avatar icon at the top right.
  3. Enter your login name or email address and password.
  4. Accept the terms and conditions.
  5. You are ready to go!

Best regards,
SAP University Alliances program

entry.title: Get Ahead of What's Next: Join the Early Release Series
entry.content:
Be First to Know What’s Next

Curious about the innovations shaping the future of supply chain? The Early Release Series gives you a front row seat. It's a biannual event where SAP product experts walk you through what's coming next in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, before everyone else.

What You'll Get Out of It

Across several days, you can join expert-led demos, gain insider insights, and take part in real-time Q&A. That means early access to new features and direct answers from the people building them. It's a practical way to see live demos, ask your questions, and plan ahead with confidence.

Sessions for the SCM Community

Here are four sessions we think you'll find valuable. Each one is a detailed, expert-led deep dive.

Artificial Intelligence — July 13, 2026, 12:30pm – 2:00pm CET

Take a broader look at where AI is heading. This session covers the Autonomous Enterprise, Joule for Business, workflow automation, and Autonomous SCM, including production, planning, and inventory agents that connect directly to your supply chain work.

Supply Chain Management — July 16, 2:00pm – 2:45pm CET

Discover the latest innovations in Supply Chain Management and see how they can support your day-to-day operations.

Manufacturing — July 14, 4:00pm – 4:45pm CET

Explore the newest manufacturing innovations, from engineering and planning to shop-floor execution. You'll see how AI-driven manufacturing agents help streamline decisions, automate routine tasks, and strengthen operational resilience. Expect practical demos, insider tips, and a clear view of what's next, so you can accelerate value from day one.

Quality Management — July 16, 4:30pm – 5:15pm CET

Get up to speed on the latest innovations in Quality Management and what they mean for your processes.

 

How to Sign Up

An active SAP Learning Hub license is required to register for the live sessions and to watch the recordings afterward.

If you're a customer:

You'll need access to at least the SAP Enterprise Support edition. Here's how to sign up:

  1. Go to the sign-up page and select Sign up.
  2. Authenticate with your S-user ID and password.
  3. The system checks your eligibility, creates your SAP Learning Hub user, and populates your learning catalogue. This step can take up to 2 hours.
  4. You'll get a confirmation e-mail once registration is complete.

If you're a partner:

With a valid SAP Universal ID and access to the Empowering SAP Partners site, you can join the live sessions and recordings directly.

Don't Miss Out

These sessions won't wait, and neither should you. Secure your spot to gain early insights, watch live demos, and stay ahead with the newest SAP updates.

Register today and be first to know what's next: learning.sap.com

entry.title: Your Path to Cloud-Native Product Data: From NX to SAP IPD
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post! In this edition, I want to outline how manufacturers and product development teams can move to a cloud-native product data strategy using the SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD) solution and its dedicated interface to NX by DSC Software AG, powered by the Product Data Integration (PDI) service.

No time to read? Register to access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert webinar via the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub here.

The Challenge: PLM in the Cloud Requires a New Integration Approach

Product lifecycle management has always depended on one critical capability: capturing engineering design data from authoring tools and feeding it reliably into downstream business processes. Material masters, bills of material, product structures, visualization – none of these can be built without a trusted source of design data.

For companies running SAP S/4HANA on-premise or private cloud, this has been handled for more than a decade by the SAP Engineering Control Center (ECTR). But as SAP customers move to the public cloud or adopt SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) solutions, they need a new integration layer built for that cloud-native reality.

That layer is SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD), and at its core sits the Product Data Integration (PDI) service: the hub that connects any CAD authoring tool to IPD, and through IPD, to the rest of the SAP ecosystem.

[Image showing the discrete industry process.]

What Is SAP IPD - and Where Does PDI Fit In?

SAP IPD is SAP platform-native PLM, built entirely on SAP BTP. It covers the full product definition lifecycle from early concept and product structure to visualization, engineering change management, and document management, and connects natively to SAP S/4HANA in any deployment: public cloud, private cloud, or on-premise.

[Image showing the architecture with SAP BTP and SAP IPD with PDI and EPS.]

Within IPD, the PDI service acts as the central integration hub for authoring tools. Engineers working in authoring systems like NX, SolidWorks, Solid Edge, Autodesk Inventor, Creo, or CATIA connect to IPD through a plugin installed in their CAD environment. That plugin exposes PDI public APIs, making it possible to save, version, check in, check out, and derive product structures, all without leaving the CAD tool.

The core design principle: engineers should never have to open an SAP screen to do their design work. The integration surface lives inside the authoring tool, and SAP receives clean, structured data automatically.

Core Capabilities: What PDI Delivers

Design Item Management with Full Versioning

Every CAD design - whether a part, assembly, or drawing - is represented in PDI as a Design Item. Design items support two levels of versioning:

Major versions are intentional: the engineer explicitly creates a new major version to mark a significant state change, such as releasing a design for downstream use or initiating a revision cycle.

Minor versions are automatic: every save action in the CAD tool creates a new minor version, capturing the complete file, metadata, and structure at that point in time. Engineers can always roll back to any prior save state if needed.

[Image showing the product data integration process.]

This is more powerful than the document info record model in traditional SAP PLM, where content versioning is limited to file content rather than the full combination of file and structure. In PDI, file and structure are always kept in sync.

Check-In / Check-Out for Controlled Collaboration

For mechanical CAD environments, PDI supports a standard check-in / check-out workflow. An engineer who wants to modify a design checks it out, reserving it so no one else can make parallel changes. Once the work is complete, they check it back in and the new state becomes available to the rest of the team. For authoring tools that support multi-user editing natively, this mechanism can be configured off.

Automatic Product Structure Derivation

When an engineer saves a CAD assembly to IPD and opts to derive the product structure, PDI automatically creates a corresponding product hierarchy in the SAP Enterprise Product Structure (EPS) - one product per design item, with the same assembly relationships. Transformation matrices, component properties, and custom attributes are all mapped from the CAD model to the product structure.

The design item structure (the D-BOM) is intentionally leaner than the full product BOM. An aerospace wing assembly may reference thousands of rivets, but PDI stores only the unique relationship, not all instances, making data storage and retrieval faster. The CAD tool knows how many instances exist; PDI does not need to duplicate that information.

Additional parts not modeled in CAD - such as lubricants or consumables - can be added directly to the EPS product structure without any impact on the design item side.

[Image showing the CAD Design Structure in the CAD system, in PDI and the product structure.]

Visualization Generated Automatically on Every Save

Whenever a design is saved and a product structure is derived, IPD automatically generates a VDS visualization file in the background. Anyone - including colleagues without a CAD license - can view the 3D product data directly inside SAP IPD through the standard visualization service. No manual export, no separate visualization workflow required.

Attribute Mapping from CAD to Product

Engineering metadata - part numbers, material designations, and custom properties defined in NX - can be mapped to product attributes in IPD using a configurable attribute mapping. Downstream processes in EPS, SAP S/4HANA, procurement, and manufacturing always have access to the authoritative engineering attributes without any manual re-entry.

Instance-level properties (properties tied to a specific use of a component within an assembly) are also supported and mapped through to the product structure.

The NX Interface: Engineering Without Context Switching

The NX integration is one of the first major mechanical CAD interfaces available on the IPD roadmap. In the NX environment, engineers see two additions to their standard interface:

The PDI Ribbon: a dedicated toolbar inside NX providing connection management, document open from IPD, save options (intermediate save or save with product derivation), check-in, and check-out actions.

The PDI Panel: a side panel embedded in NX displaying live IPD data for the active design including design item number, major and minor version, status, associated product, and file location. Engineers can switch between detail view, tree view, and search, all from within NX without opening a browser or a separate SAP Fiori app.

The design experience is intentionally native. Engineers create assemblies, modify parts, create drawings, and use the reuse library exactly as they would without the integration. The PDI layer intercepts save actions and handles synchronization to IPD transparently.

[Image showing the visualization in NX and PDI.]

Architecture: Cloud-Native, Clean Core, and Broadly Compatible

The PDI service runs on SAP BTP and exposes public APIs that any authoring tool can connect to, not just SAP-listed tools. Third-party partners can develop their own integrations using the published PDI APIs, making the architecture open by design.

Connectivity to SAP S/4HANA is flexible: IPD on BTP integrates with SAP S/4HANA in any deployment model - on-premise, private cloud, or public cloud. Companies do not need to move their ERP to the public cloud before adopting IPD for PLM.

For customers who still run ECTR with SAP S/4HANA on-premise or private cloud: that solution remains fully supported for as long as SAP S/4HANA exists. IPD and PDI are the path forward for public cloud and BTP-native scenarios - additive, not a forced migration.

What Is Coming Next: The 2026 Roadmap

The integration roadmap for PDI covers several major milestones through the rest of 2026:

  • Q2 2026 - Released: Autodesk Inventor, Solid Edge, and SOLIDWORKS integrations are now available.
  • Q3 2026 - Planned: Connecting NX Design solutions to design items and product structures
  • Q4 2026 - Planned: Integration of design items to IPD Document Management and Change Management.

Conclusion

The move to cloud-native PLM does not have to mean a painful redesign of engineering workflows. With SAP IPD and the PDI service, companies can bring their existing CAD-centric design processes into a modern BTP-native architecture with automatic versioning, live product structure derivation, integrated visualization, and clean downstream connectivity to SAP S/4HANA.

Engineers stay in their tools. Product managers get structured, current data. And the business gets a single, trustworthy source of product truth - without spreadsheets, manual uploads, or reconciliation gaps between design and manufacturing.

More Information

- SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub: https://www.sap.com/cmp/wlp/sap-supply-chain-management-enablement/index.html#plm

- SAP Integrated Product Development on SAP Community: https://community.sap.com/topics/integrated-product-development

- SAP IPD Roadmap: Roadmap Explorer

- DSC Software AG: www.dsc.de

 Don't hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date with our latest enablement assets:
https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q2-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
Welcome to the Q2 2026 edition of our SAP Green Ledger Quarterly Release Highlights! This blog summarizes the most important SAP Green Ledger innovations and enhancements delivered during Q2 2026. Highlights include new capabilities for carbon traceability, carbon collection processing, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), carbon allocations, analytics, and usability improvements across SAP Green Ledger.

Read on to learn more about the key innovations introduced throughout Q2 2026:

  • Carbon Traceability with Related SAP S/4HANA Documents
  • Mass Reversal of Carbon Collections
  • Simplified CBAM Processing
  • Flexible Carbon Allocations
  • Analytical Dashboard Update
  • Further Updates and Enhancements

 

Carbon Traceability with Related SAP S/4HANA Documents

The Manage Carbon Collections app now includes a new "Related Documents" section that allows you to maintain and view references to the SAP S/4HANA documents from which emissions originate. Supported reference types include:

  • Purchasing Documents
  • Accounting Documents
  • Other Documents

If you import material movements into SAP Green Ledger from a connected SAP S/4HANA system, related document references are transferred automatically. When importing carbon data via CSV files, you can also include related document information in the file. For manually created carbon collections, you can maintain related documents directly in the Manage Carbon Collections app. These references are then carried through from the carbon collection into the corresponding journal entries in the Manage Journal Entries app. This allows you to trace carbon postings throughout the accounting process, from the originating business documents to the resulting carbon journal entries, also improving auditability of carbon data.

Related Documents in a Carbon CollectionTo further simplify navigation and reporting, you can now also filter in the Manage Carbon Collections, Manage Journal Entries and Display Line Items apps by Related Document Type and Related Document ID. This helps you quickly locate carbon records for specific business transactions. For more details check SAP Help: Create a Carbon Collection and Use a CSV Template.

 

Mass Reversal of Carbon Collections

The Manage Carbon Collections app now supports reversing multiple posted carbon collections in a single step. Instead of reversing each carbon collection individually, you can now select and reverse multiple posted carbon collections at once. This significantly reduces manual effort, particularly after correcting mass uploads. Up to 5,000 carbon collections can be reversed in a single operation. For more information, see SAP Help: Post a Carbon Collection to the Journal.

 

Simplified CBAM Processing

Several enhancements improve the processing and monitoring of Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) carbon collections while also increasing transparency for CBAM accounts configuration.

Efficient Processing of CBAM Carbon Collections

The Manage Carbon Collections app now also supports reversing CBAM carbon collections. In addition, you can now post and reverse multiple CBAM carbon collections in a single step. As a result, you can process large volumes of carbon collections that require carbon certificates more efficiently.

When reversing CBAM carbon collections, the resulting journal entries are created with the journal entry source type Carbon Collection Reversal, providing a clear audit trail for correction postings. When posting or reversing multiple CBAM carbon collections, SAP Green Ledger automatically calculates a single fair value per company code. This reduces manual effort and ensures consistent valuation across company codes.

To support the monitoring of these postings, the Manage Carbon Liabilities app now supports filtering carbon liabilities by Posted and Reversed status. With that, you can quickly monitor the processing status of carbon liabilities and identify relevant entries. Check out SAP Help for more details: Post a Carbon Collection to the Journal, Fair Value Calculation, and View Carbon Liabilities.

Filter for Posted and Reversed Carbon Liabilities in the Manage Carbon Liabilities App

Improved Visibility into EU CBAM Accounts by Company Code

The Configure Carbon Certificate Data app now displays the EU CBAM accounts maintained in the Determine Mandatory Accounts app for each company code. This allows you to view the configured account assignments directly while working with carbon certificate data, without switching between applications. See also SAP Help: Configure Carbon Certificate Data and Determine Mandatory Accounts.

 EU CBAM Accounts per Company Code in Configure Carbon Certificate Data App

 

Flexible Carbon Allocations

Several enhancements make carbon allocations more flexible and align them more closely with financial accounting. Our new capabilities can help you reuse existing allocation logic and support project-based allocation scenarios.

Copy Financial Flow for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

The ability to create allocation models by copying financial flows in the Manage Allocations app is now also available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Previously available only for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, this enhancement enables you to create carbon allocation models based on the allocation patterns already used in your connected SAP S/4HANA system.

Allocation Model Copied from Financial Flow

Using the latest Cost Center Accounting data from the connected SAP S/4HANA system, SAP Green Ledger automatically copies the receiving objects and allocation percentages and transfers them into the allocation model. This eliminates the need to manually maintain allocation rules and helps keep carbon allocations aligned with financial allocations. For more details, check SAP Help: Create Allocation Models by Copying from Financial Flow.

Project-Level Carbon Allocations with WBS Elements

The Manage Allocations app now supports Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements as both sender and receiver dimensions when creating periodic allocation models. For both the sender and receiver, you can now choose a WBS element or a cost center as the allocation dimension, enabling allocation scenarios such as WBS-to-WBS, WBS-to-Cost Center, Cost Center-to-WBS, and Cost Center-to-Cost Center. To ensure consistency with the existing allocation behavior for cost centers, the corresponding Profit Center and Segment are now automatically derived as well when a WBS element is used as the receiver dimension.

WBS Elements Used as Sender and Receiver Dimensions in a Periodic Allocation Model

 To further improve transparency, Partner WBS Elements are now included in journal entry line items and displayed in both the Manage Journal Entries and Display Line Items apps. You can also filter, group, and sort by Partner WBS Element in the Display Line Items app, giving you better visibility into carbon allocation flows across project structures. For more details, see Create Period Allocation Models.

 

Analytical Dashboard Update

The SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard has also been updated for Q2 2026 with the latest analytical capabilities for combined financial and carbon reporting. For a detailed overview of these enhancements, see the dedicated SAP Community blog post and the SAP Help Portal topic Analyze Carbon Data Alongside Financial Data.

 

Further Updates and Enhancements

Several smaller enhancements further improve the usability of SAP Green Ledger.

Improved Journal Entry Usability

The Manage Journal Entries app now allows you to maximize the Line Items table. You can review journal entries with many line items more comfortably and reduce the need for scrolling. Additionally, each line item is now assigned a stable unique identifier that remains unchanged when the table is sorted or filtered. The stable identifier makes it easier to reference individual line items and improves navigation during journal analysis.

Updated Posting Terminology

The posting action in the Manage Carbon Collections app has been renamed from Post Carbon Collection to simply Post. The related posting statuses are:

  • Posting
  • Posted
  • Posting Failed
New Origin Business System Filter

A new Origin Business System filter is now available in the Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances apps. Selecting an origin business system automatically limits the available master data to values from that system. Likewise, when a master data object that is linked to a specific business system, such as a company code, is selected, the corresponding business system is identified automatically and applied to the remaining filters.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
SAP Developer Challenge - Week 1 Submission

Here is my submission for the Week 1 Developer Challenge (Snake & Ladder Game):

1. db/schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
  Disconnected; // Player lost connection or automatically skip turns without breaking the session context.
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

2. srv/game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @to: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @to: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @to: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @to: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

3. GameService Endpoints Screenshot

Below is the screenshot showing the endpoints loaded at http://localhost:4004:

4. TurnStatus Choice Explanation

I introduced the custom Disconnected status value to represent players losing their connection, allowing the state machine to trigger AI takeovers or automatically skip turns without breaking the session context.
