entry.title: SAP SD EDI ORDERS (850) Interface Configuration - Complete Setup Guide
entry.content:
1. Overview and Architecture

The EDI 850 Purchase Order transaction is one of the most critical electronic commerce documents exchanged between trading partners. In SAP Sales & Distribution (SD), inbound EDI 850 messages are processed through the IDoc (Intermediate Document) framework, converting the raw EDI data into SAP sales orders automatically.

 

1.1  End-to-End Process Flow

The following diagram describes the complete path of an inbound EDI 850 from the trading partner to the SAP sales order:

 

Step 1

Trading Partner transmits EDI 850 file via AS2, FTP/SFTP, VAN, or HTTPS

External

Step 2

EDI Subsystem / Middleware (e.g., SAP PI/PO, IBM Sterling, MuleSoft) receives and maps EDI → ORDERS05 IDoc XML

Middleware

Step 3

IDoc posted to SAP via RFC (tRFC/qRFC) or file interface

Interface

Step 4

SAP IDoc Inbound Processing (WE02/WE05) validates and routes the IDoc

SAP

Step 5

Function module IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS called; Partner profile checked

SAP

Step 6

Sales Order created in SD via transaction VA01 logic

SAP SD

Step 7

Functional ACK (997/999) or Application ACK (855) returned to partner

Outbound

 

1.2  Key SAP Components

Component

Transaction/Object

Purpose

IDoc Type

ORDERS05

Standard IDoc type for inbound purchase orders

Message Type

ORDERS

Links IDoc type to processing function module

Partner Profile

WE20

Trading partner-specific EDI configuration

Port Definition

WE21

Communication channel (file port, tRFC port)

Process Code

ORDE

Inbound processing logic for sales orders

Function Module

IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS

Core FM that creates sales orders from IDocs

Logical System

BD54

Identifies sending/receiving systems

Distribution Model

BD64

ALE distribution model for message routing

 

NOTE: SAP S/4HANA uses the same IDoc framework but may leverage additional APIs (OData/BAPI) for order creation. This guide applies to both SAP ECC 6.0 and S/4HANA environments.

 

 

2. Prerequisites and System Requirements

2.1  SAP Basis Configuration

Before configuring the EDI 850 interface, ensure the following Basis prerequisites are in place:

 

Prerequisite

Transaction/Path

Verification

RFC Destinations configured

SM59

Check connection to middleware system

Logical System names defined

BD54

At least one LS for sending partner

ALE Distribution Model active

BD64

Model contains ORDERS message type

IDoc Port defined

WE21

File or tRFC port created and tested

Number ranges for IDocs

WE03 / SNRO

Object EDIDC with sufficient range

Workflow active (optional)

SWI5 / SWDD

For error notification routing

 

2.2  SD Master Data Requirements

The following master data must exist before purchase orders can be processed successfully:

 

Master Data

Transaction

Key Fields

Notes

Customer Master

XD01/VD01

Account Group, Sales Area, Partner Functions

Bill-to, Ship-to, Sold-to must be linked

Sales Organization

SPRO

SOrg / Dist Channel / Division

Must match IDoc header data

Material Master

MM01

Sales Org 1&2, MRP, Plant views

MVKE and MARA must be active

Customer-Material Info

VD51

Customer mat. number, UOM

Required if EAN/GTIN mapping used

Pricing Conditions

VK11

PR00, customer-specific prices

For automatic price determination

Output Determination

NACE

Output types V1, V2, V3

Required for outbound EDI 855/856

Shipping Point

SPRO > LE

Shipping Point determination

Required for delivery scheduling

Plant Assignment

SPRO > SD

Delivering plant rules

Drives MRP and availability check

 

2.3  Required SAP Authorizations

The configuration user and the batch user executing inbound processing require specific authorization objects:

 

Authorization Object

Required Values / Activities

S_IDOC_ALL

Activity: 01 (Create), 02 (Change), 03 (Display) – Full IDoc access

B_ALE_RECV

RCVPRT = LS, RCVPRN = <Logical System>

V_VBAK_AAT

AUART = Sales Order Types (e.g., OR, ZOR)

V_VBAK_VKO

Sales Organization / Distribution Channel / Division

S_RFC

RFC_TYPE = FUGR, RFC_NAME = EDIE*, IDOC*

S_DEVELOP

Only for debugging – restrict in Production

 

IMPORTANT: Always use a dedicated technical user (not a dialog user) for IDoc inbound processing batch jobs. Assign only the minimum required authorizations.

 

 

3. IDoc Configuration (WE20 / WE21)

3.1  Port Definition (WE21)

Ports define the technical communication channel between SAP and the external EDI system. Two port types are commonly used for EDI 850 processing:

 

3.1.1  File Port Configuration

Use file ports when the middleware delivers IDocs as XML or flat files to a shared directory:

 

Parameter

Configuration Value

Port Name

EDIFILE (user-defined, up to 10 chars)

Port Type

File

Inbound File

/usr/sap/trans/edi/inbound/orders*.xml

Outbound File

/usr/sap/trans/edi/outbound/

IDoc XML Version

3.0 (recommended for S/4HANA)

Function Module

EDI_PATH_CREATE_CLIENT_DOCNUM (standard)

Status File

/usr/sap/trans/edi/status/

 

3.1.2  tRFC Port Configuration

Use tRFC ports for direct RFC-based IDoc posting from middleware systems (e.g., SAP PI/PO):

 

Parameter

Configuration Value

Port Name

SAPMIDDLE (user-defined)

Port Type

Transactional RFC (tRFC)

RFC Destination

MIDDLEWRE_SYS (as defined in SM59)

Description

tRFC Port to PI/PO Middleware System

IDoc packet size

1 (recommended for error isolation)

 

3.2  Partner Profile Setup (WE20)

The Partner Profile is the heart of SAP EDI configuration. It defines how SAP exchanges IDocs with each specific trading partner. Each customer who sends EDI 850s requires their own partner profile entry.

 

3.2.1  Partner Profile Header

Field

Value / Description

Partner Number

<Customer Number from KNA1 – e.g., 1000001>

Partner Type

KU (Customer)

Partner Class

01 (Standard)

Agent (Type/No.)

US / <User ID for error notifications>

Language

EN

Post Immediately

Checked (for automatic processing)

 

3.2.2  Inbound Parameters (Message Type ORDERS)

Navigate to the Inbound Parameters tab in WE20 and add the following entry:

 

Field

Value

Field

Value

Message Type

ORDERS

IDoc Type (Basic)

ORDERS05

Process Code

ORDE

Cancel Processing After

Errors (recommended)

Trigger Immediately

Checked

Background Processing

Unchecked

Date/Time Conversion

Inbound (trading partner TZ)

Ignore Syntax Errors

NOT checked (production)

 

WARNING: Never check 'Ignore Syntax Errors' in Production environments. This masks data quality issues and can lead to incorrect sales orders being created.

 

3.2.3  Outbound Parameters (ACK – Message Type ORDRSP)

If sending Application Acknowledgments (855 – Purchase Order Acknowledgment) back to the trading partner, configure outbound parameters:

 

Field

Value

Message Type

ORDRSP

IDoc Type

ORDERS05

Output Mode

Transfer IDoc Immediately

Port

SAPMIDDLE (or file port)

Pack. Size

1

Basic Type

ORDRSP05

 

3.3  Message Control (NACE / WE40)

Message control determines when and how IDocs are triggered in outbound direction. For inbound 850 processing, configure output type EDI1 in the sales order output determination procedure:

 

Output Type

Description / Use

EDI1

Order Confirmation output (triggers 855 ORDRSP)

EDI2

Order Change output (triggers 860)

LALE

ALE distribution (internal system-to-system)

RD00

Invoice output (for 810 outbound)

 

 

4. EDI 850 to ORDERS05 IDoc Segment Mapping

This section provides the complete mapping between EDI X12 850 segments and their corresponding ORDERS05 IDoc segments. This mapping is implemented either in the middleware (SAP PI/PO, Sterling, MuleSoft) or in an ABAP enhancement user exit.

 

4.1  Header Segments

EDI 850 Header → E1EDK01 / E1EDK14 / E1EDKT1

 

EDI 850 Segment

Element

IDoc Segment / Field

Notes / Logic

BEG

01 (Trans Purpose)

E1EDK01-BSART

Map to SAP order type: '00'→OR, '01'→RE

BEG

02 (Order Type)

E1EDK01-BSART

Cross-reference to SAP document type

BEG

03 (PO Number)

E1EDK02-BELNR (QUALF=001)

Customer purchase order number

BEG

05 (PO Date)

E1EDK02-DATUM (QUALF=001)

Format: CCYYMMDD in IDoc

CUR

02 (Currency Code)

E1EDK01-CURCY

ISO 4217 currency code

DTM

01=002 / 02 (Date)

E1EDK03-DATUM (IDDAT=002)

Requested delivery date

DTM

01=010 / 02 (Date)

E1EDK03-DATUM (IDDAT=012)

Shipment date

FOB

01 (Shipment Terms)

E1EDK01-INCO1 / INCO2

Incoterms: map to SAP Incoterms table

ITD

01 (Payment Terms)

E1EDK01-ZTERM

Cross-ref to SAP payment terms key

TD5

04 (Carrier)

E1EDK01-VSBED

Shipping conditions / carrier code

 

4.2  Name and Address Segments (N1 Loop)

 

EDI 850 Segment

Element

IDoc Segment / Field

Notes / Logic

N1

01=ST (Ship-To)

E1EDKA1-PARVW=WE

Partner function WE (Ship-to Party)

N1

01=BT (Bill-To)

E1EDKA1-PARVW=RE

Partner function RE (Bill-to Party)

N1

01=BY (Buyer)

E1EDKA1-PARVW=AG

Partner function AG (Sold-to Party)

N1

02 (Name)

E1EDKA1-NAME1

Party name – line 1

N3

01 (Address 1)

E1EDKA1-STRAS

Street address

N3

02 (Address 2)

E1EDKA1-NAME3

Address supplement

N4

01 (City)

E1EDKA1-ORT01

City name

N4

02 (State)

E1EDKA1-REGIO

State/Region code

N4

03 (ZIP)

E1EDKA1-PSTLZ

Postal code

N4

04 (Country)

E1EDKA1-LAND1

ISO country code

REF

01=DP / 02 (Dept No)

E1EDKA1-ABTEI

Department number / Buyer dept

PER

02 (Contact Name)

E1EDKA1-ANSPN

Contact person name

PER

04 (Phone)

E1EDKA1-TELF1

Contact phone number

 

4.3  Line Item Segments (PO1 Loop)

 

EDI 850 Segment

Element

IDoc Segment / Field

Notes / Logic

PO1

01 (Line No.)

E1EDP01-POSEX

Item number – pad to 6 digits

PO1

02 (Qty Ordered)

E1EDP01-MENGE

Ordered quantity

PO1

03 (Unit of Measure)

E1EDP01-MENEE

X12 UOM → SAP UOM cross-ref

PO1

04 (Unit Price)

E1EDP05-KRATE

Price per PREIS unit

PO1

05 (Price Basis)

E1EDP05-KPEIN

Price basis quantity

PO1

07 (Product ID – VP)

E1EDP19-IDTNR (QUALF=001)

Customer part number

PO1

07 (Product ID – EN)

E1EDP19-IDTNR (QUALF=EN)

EAN-13 / GTIN-13 barcode

PO1

07 (Product ID – IN)

E1EDP19-IDTNR (QUALF=002)

Supplier (SAP) material number

PID

05 (Description)

E1EDPT1-TDLINE

Item description text

REF

01=DP / 02

E1EDP01-POSEX

Buyer's line item reference

DTM

01=002 / 02 (Date)

E1EDP20-EDATU

Line-level requested delivery date

SCH

02 (Qty)

E1EDP20-WMENG

Schedule line quantity

SCH

04 (Ship Date)

E1EDP20-EDATU

Schedule line delivery date

SAC

05 (Allowance/Charge)

E1EDP05-BETRG

Surcharges / discounts at line

 

4.4  ORDERS05 IDoc Structure – Key Segments

Reference guide to the key ORDERS05 segments and their purpose in the SAP SD order:

 

IDoc Segment

Purpose in SAP SD

E1EDK01

Document header – order type, currency, total value

E1EDK14

Header organization data – sales org, dist channel, division

E1EDKA1

Partner functions – sold-to, ship-to, bill-to, payer

E1EDK02

Reference documents – customer PO number, dates

E1EDK03

Date segments – requested delivery date, document date

E1EDKT1

Header texts – special instructions, delivery notes

E1EDP01

Line item header – item number, material, quantity, UOM

E1EDP19

Material identification – customer mat#, EAN, GTIN

E1EDP05

Line item pricing – price, per unit, currency

E1EDP20

Delivery schedule per line item

E1EDPT1

Line item texts – item-level special instructions

 

 

5. ABAP / User Exit Configuration

SAP provides standard user exits (BAdIs and Function Module exits) to handle custom business logic during inbound IDoc processing. These are essential for most real-world implementations.

 

5.1  Key User Exits and BAdIs

Exit / BAdI

Purpose

EXIT_SAPLVEDA_001 (MV45AFZZ)

Modify sales order header data before save

EXIT_SAPLVEDA_002 (MV45AFZZ)

Modify sales order item data before save

USEREXIT_MOVE_FIELD_TO_VBAK

Transfer custom fields to order header (VBAK)

USEREXIT_MOVE_FIELD_TO_VBAP

Transfer custom fields to order items (VBAP)

BAdI: ORDERS_BADI

General BAdI for ORDERS IDoc processing

BAdI: BADI_SD_SALES_BAPI

Enhance BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2

BAdI: LE_SHP_DELIVERY_PROC

Influence delivery processing triggered by order

IDOC_INPUT_ORDERS (enhancement)

Pre/post-processing around standard FM

 

5.2  Common Customizations via User Exits

5.2.1  Material Number Cross-Reference

Trading partners send their own part numbers in PO1-07 (qualifier VP). A cross-reference lookup converts this to the SAP material number:

 

FORM userexit_move_field_to_vbap.

  " Read customer material from MARA/MVKE via AUART table

  SELECT SINGLE matnr INTO vbap-matnr

    FROM mvke WHERE vkorg = vbak-vkorg

           AND  vtweg = vbak-vtweg

           AND  kdmat = xvbap-kdmat.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.

    " Try customer-material info record (KNMT)

    SELECT SINGLE matnr INTO vbap-matnr FROM knmt

      WHERE kunnr = vbak-kunnr AND kdmat = xvbap-kdmat.

  ENDIF.

ENDFORM.

 

5.2.2  UOM Conversion

EDI X12 UOM codes differ from SAP internal units. A conversion table (Z-table or standard T006A) must translate codes such as CA→CS (Case), EA→ST (Each), DZ→DZ (Dozen):

 

EDI X12 UOM Code

SAP Internal UOM

EA (Each)

ST

CA (Case)

CS

DZ (Dozen)

DZ

BX (Box)

BX

PK (Package)

PCE

LB (Pound)

LB

KG (Kilogram)

KG

FT (Foot)

FT

MT (Meter)

M

 

5.3  Error Handling Configuration

Proper error handling ensures failed IDocs are visible, actionable, and do not silently drop orders:

 

Error Scenario

Recommended Handling

Material not found

Set IDoc status 51 (Application Error) – do not create order

Customer not found / inactive

Set status 51 – alert EDI team via workflow WS20000075

Duplicate PO number

Check VBKD-BSTKD; if exists, reject with clear error message

Invalid delivery date (past)

Apply tolerance check; default to next working day if within tolerance

Price discrepancy > threshold

Create order with pricing condition blocked (billing block Z1)

Missing required segment

Status 26 (Syntax Error) – return 997 error to trading partner

Quantity below minimum order

Create with warning; alert customer service via output message

Unavailable plant / storage loc

Create order; trigger availability check with substitute plant rules

 

 

6. Testing Procedure

6.1  Unit Testing – IDoc Inbound Simulation

Use transaction WE19 to test inbound IDoc processing without requiring a live EDI feed:

 

  1. Go to WE19 (Test Tool for IDoc Processing)
  2. Enter an existing IDoc number or create from scratch using the template option
  3. Select 'Standard Inbound Processing' and choose process code ORDE
  4. Modify the IDoc data as needed in the editor (customer PO#, material, qty, date)
  5. Execute (F8) – the system will process the IDoc and display the result
  6. Check the created sales order in VA03 using the reference in the IDoc status record
  7. Verify all key fields: material, qty, price, delivery date, partner functions

 

6.2  IDoc Monitoring Transactions

Transaction

Purpose

WE02

IDoc List – filter by partner, message type, status, date range

WE05

IDoc Overview – graphical status display with drill-down

BD87

Status Monitor – reprocess failed IDocs (status 51, 52, 64, 65)

WE07

IDoc Statistics – volume reporting by partner and message type

WE09

Search IDocs by content (segment field values)

WE10

Display IDocs from archive

SM58

tRFC Monitor – check failed RFC IDoc postings

SM50/SM66

Work Process Monitor – check for stuck IDoc processing jobs

 

6.3  IDoc Status Code Reference

The following status codes are critical for troubleshooting inbound ORDERS processing:

 

Status

Description

Action Required

02

Error passing IDoc data to port

Check port definition in WE21; file system permissions

05

Error during translation

Check middleware mapping; segment syntax

26

Error during syntax check

Validate IDoc against ORDERS05 structure in WE30

51

Application document not posted

Most common! Check error message in IDoc status records

52

Application document partially posted

Review partial order; reprocess after fixing root cause

53

Application document posted

SUCCESS – Sales order created successfully

56

IDoc with errors added from outbound

Partner profile issue; check WE20 outbound parameters

64

IDoc is ready to be transferred to application

IDoc queued; check background job RBDAPP01

65

Error in ALE service

ALE distribution model issue; check BD64

 

6.4  End-to-End Integration Test Checklist

 

Test Scenario

Expected Result / Verification

Happy Path: Valid 850 with all required segments

Sales order created in status Open; IDoc status 53

Customer material number cross-reference

Correct SAP material appears on order line item

Multiple line items (>10 lines)

All lines created; item numbers sequential and correct

Requested delivery date in future

Delivery date set correctly on all line items

Requested delivery date in past

Order blocked or date adjusted per tolerance rule

Duplicate PO number for same customer

Duplicate detected; IDoc rejected with status 51

Invalid / inactive material

IDoc error; no order created; alert generated

UOM conversion (e.g., EA to ST)

SAP UOM ST on order; quantity unchanged

Special characters in text fields

Characters stored correctly; no encoding errors

ACK (997/855) returned to partner

Outbound IDoc visible in WE02 with status 03

Large order (100+ line items)

Performance acceptable; all lines processed correctly

Cancel/change request (860 follow-up)

Not in scope for 850 – handled by separate interface

 

 

7. Production Configuration and Monitoring

7.1  Background Jobs for IDoc Processing

Schedule the following background jobs in SM36 to ensure automatic processing of inbound IDocs:

 

Job Name

Program

Frequency

Description

ZEDI_RBDAPP01

RBDAPP01

Every 15 min

Process IDocs in status 64 (ready to post)

ZEDI_RBDMANIN

RBDMANIN

Every 30 min

Re-process manually released IDocs

ZEDI_RBDMOIND

RBDMOIND

Hourly

IDoc statistics update / monitoring

ZEDI_RSEOUT00

RSEOUT00

Every 5 min

Send outbound IDocs (997 ACK / 855)

ZEDI_RBDCPCLR

RBDCPCLR

Weekly (weekend)

Clean up processed change pointer records

ZEDI_SARJADMIN

SARJADMIN

Daily (midnight)

Archive processed IDocs older than 90 days

 

7.2  Performance Tuning

For high-volume EDI 850 environments (>10,000 orders/day), apply the following performance optimizations:

 

Optimization Area

Recommendation

Parallel processing

Enable parallel processing in RBDAPP01 (max 6 parallel processes)

IDoc packet size

Set packet size to 10-50 IDocs per session for batch processing

Database indexes

Ensure custom Z-tables have proper indexes on key search fields

Buffer settings

Buffer cross-reference tables (ZTEDI_MATNR_XREF) to reduce DB reads

Work process allocation

Reserve dedicated background WPs for EDI processing (SM50)

qRFC vs tRFC

Use qRFC inbound queues for guaranteed exactly-once delivery

Availability check

Consider deactivating ATP for EDI orders if volume is very high

Partner grouping

Group smaller trading partners on shared batch jobs; large partners alone

 

7.3  Alerting and Escalation

Configure proactive monitoring to detect EDI failures before they impact order fulfillment:

 

Alert Trigger

Action / Escalation

IDoc count in status 51 > 5

Auto-email to EDI team (SCOT email output)

No IDocs received from partner in >2 hours during business hours

Call to partner EDI contact; check connectivity

Error rate > 2% of daily volume

Escalate to EDI Manager; review mapping changes

Background job RBDAPP01 fails or runs long

Alert Basis team; check SM37 and SM50

tRFC queue (SM58) grows > 100 entries

Check middleware system connectivity; alert Basis

Sales order creation failure rate > 1%

Alert SD functional team; check master data integrity

 

7.4  Common Transaction Quick Reference

Transaction

Use

Typical User

WE02

IDoc list and monitoring

EDI Analyst / Support

WE05

Graphical IDoc overview

EDI Analyst

BD87

Reprocess failed IDocs

EDI Analyst / Support

WE19

Test IDoc processing

Developer / Analyst

WE20

Maintain partner profiles

EDI Functional Lead

WE21

Maintain ports

Basis / EDI Lead

WE30

IDoc type documentation

Developer

WE31

Segment documentation

Developer

VA03

Display sales order

SD/EDI Support

SM37

Background job monitor

Basis / EDI Support

SM59

RFC destination display

Basis

BD54

Logical systems

Basis / EDI Lead

 

 

8. Troubleshooting Guide

8.1  Diagnostic Decision Tree

When an EDI 850 fails to create a sales order, follow this structured diagnostic approach:

 

STEP 1: Check WE02 or WE05 for the IDoc. Confirm it was received. If not, the issue is upstream (middleware/connectivity, not SAP).

 

STEP 2: Review IDoc status. Status 26 = syntax/structure issue. Status 51 = application error. Status 64 = not yet processed (check RBDAPP01 job).

 

STEP 3: Double-click the IDoc status record to view the detailed error message. This usually pinpoints the exact cause.

 

STEP 4: Check the partner profile in WE20 – ensure the customer number, partner type (KU), and message type (ORDERS) exist with correct process code (ORDE).

 

STEP 5: For material errors, verify MARA/MVKE, customer-material info (VD51), and the cross-reference Z-table. For customer errors, check XD03.

 

8.2  Error Message Reference

Error Message

Root Cause & Resolution

No partner profile for partner XXXX / KU

Partner profile (WE20) missing. Create entry for customer number with message type ORDERS.

Error in partner determination for ORDERS

Check partner function configuration in customizing (VOV8 / partner determination procedure).

Material XXXX not found for sales org YYYY

Material not extended to the sales organization. Use MM01 to extend or correct cross-reference.

Delivery date YYYYMMDD is in the past

Requested delivery date from 850 is historical. Configure tolerance or default rule in user exit.

No pricing condition found for item X

Pricing condition PR00 missing for material/customer combination. Maintain in VK11.

Duplicate purchase order number XXXX

Customer already submitted this PO. Investigate with trading partner; may need to void and resubmit.

Customer XXXX has deletion flag set

Customer master is marked for deletion. Unset deletion flag in XD02 or use correct customer.

Division XXXX not maintained for item X

Material MVKE division doesn't match sales area. Correct material master or IDoc division field.

Error in availability check for item X

ATP configuration issue or plant not assigned. Check plant determination and MRP settings.

Account assignment mandatory for item X

CO object required. Configure account assignment category in customizing (VOV6).

 

8.3  Reprocessing Failed IDocs

To reprocess an IDoc that failed with status 51 or 52 after correcting the root cause:

 

  1. Correct the underlying data issue (master data, partner profile, etc.)
  2. Go to BD87 (Status Monitor for ALE Messages)
  3. Select 'Inbound IDoc' and filter by Partner Number and Message Type ORDERS
  4. Set status filter to 51 (Application document not posted)
  5. Select the relevant IDocs and click 'Execute' to trigger reprocessing
  6. Monitor result in WE02 – IDocs should move to status 53 (posted successfully)

 

TIP: If a large number of IDocs need correction (e.g., mass material change), consider using WE19 to test one corrected IDoc before running BD87 for all.

 

 

9. Compliance, Security, and Governance

9.1  Data Privacy Considerations

EDI 850 transactions contain sensitive business and personal data. Ensure the following controls are in place:

 

  • IDoc archive data must be retained per contractual and legal requirements (typically 7-10 years)
  • PII in name/address segments (N1/N3/N4) must be handled per GDPR / CCPA requirements
  • EDI transmission must be encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+ for AS2/HTTPS; SFTP for file-based)
  • AS2 certificates must be renewed before expiry; monitor certificate validity dates
  • Implement non-repudiation via AS2 MDN (Message Disposition Notification)
  • Restrict access to partner profile maintenance (WE20) to authorized EDI functional leads only

 

9.2  Change Management

All changes to the EDI 850 interface configuration must follow the standard change management process:

 

Change Type

Process / Approvals Required

New trading partner onboarding

EDI Lead approval + SD functional sign-off + UAT + Go-live checklist

Partner profile changes (WE20)

EDI Lead approval + change ticket; mandatory regression test

Segment mapping changes

Full regression test with trading partner; EDI Lead + IT Manager approval

User exit / ABAP changes

Full SDLC process: dev → QA → UAT → Production transport

Schedule / job changes

Basis lead approval; inform EDI operations team

Port definition changes

Basis lead + EDI lead co-approval; connectivity test required

 

9.3  Trading Partner Onboarding Checklist

Use this checklist when onboarding a new trading partner for EDI 850 Purchase Orders:

 

Task

Owner

Obtain Trading Partner EDI questionnaire (ISA ID, GS ID, test/prod AS2 parameters)

EDI Analyst

Create/verify Customer Master in SAP (XD01) with all required partner functions

SD Master Data

Set up AS2 connection in middleware (certificates, URLs, MDN settings)

EDI Technical

Configure partner profile in WE20 (Inbound ORDERS + Outbound ORDRSP)

EDI Functional

Set up material/UOM cross-reference table entries for partner

EDI Analyst

Run Unit Test: WE19 test with sample IDoc; verify sales order creation

EDI Analyst

Perform Integration Test: live 850 from partner system in sandbox/QA

EDI Analyst + Partner

Validate 997 Functional ACK returned to partner correctly

EDI Analyst

Validate 855 Application ACK (if applicable) returned correctly

EDI Analyst

Obtain sign-off from trading partner and business stakeholder

Project Manager

Document partner profile settings and any custom mapping rules

EDI Analyst

Schedule go-live date and communicate to all stakeholders

Project Manager

Monitor first 48 hours of production traffic closely

EDI Operations

 

 

10. Appendix

10.1  Related SAP Transactions – Complete Reference

Transaction

Module

Description

WE20

EDI

Partner Profile Maintenance

WE21

EDI

Port Definition

WE30

EDI

IDoc Type Documentation

WE31

EDI

Segment Documentation

WE60

EDI

IDoc Type Display (Graphical)

WE19

EDI

Test Tool for IDoc Processing

WE02

EDI

IDoc List

WE05

EDI

IDoc Overview

WE09

EDI

Search IDocs by Content

BD87

ALE

Status Monitor – Reprocess IDocs

BD54

ALE

Logical Systems

BD64

ALE

Distribution Model

SM59

Basis

RFC Destinations

SM36

Basis

Schedule Background Jobs

SM37

Basis

Background Job Monitor

SM58

Basis

tRFC Monitor

SCOT

Basis

SAPconnect (Email/Fax output)

VA01

SD

Create Sales Order

VA02

SD

Change Sales Order

VA03

SD

Display Sales Order

XD01

SD/FI

Create Customer (Centrally)

VD01

SD

Create Customer (Sales Area)

VD51

SD

Customer-Material Information Record

VK11

SD

Maintain Pricing Conditions

MM01

MM

Create Material Master

NACE

SD/MM

Output Determination Customizing

SPRO

IMG

SAP Customizing Project

 

10.2  EDI X12 850 – Mandatory Segment Summary

Segment

Segment Name

ISA

Interchange Control Header – EDI envelope identification

GS

Functional Group Header – transaction set grouping

ST

Transaction Set Header – start of 850 transaction

BEG

Beginning Segment for Purchase Order – PO number, date, type

CUR

Currency – currency code for the order

REF

Reference Identification – additional reference numbers

DTM

Date/Time Reference – delivery dates, requested ship dates

N1

Name – party identification (Buyer, Ship-To, Bill-To)

N3

Address Information – street address

N4

Geographic Location – city, state, ZIP, country

PO1

Baseline Item Data – line number, qty, UOM, price, material ID

PID

Product/Item Description – text description of item

DTM

Date/Time Reference (at line level) – delivery date per line

CTT

Transaction Totals – total line count

SE

Transaction Set Trailer – end of 850 transaction

GE

Functional Group Trailer

IEA

Interchange Control Trailer

 

10.3  Glossary

Term

Definition

ALE

Application Link Enabling – SAP framework for distributing IDocs between SAP systems

AS2

Applicability Statement 2 – secure HTTPS-based EDI transmission protocol

ATP

Available-to-Promise – SAP availability check during order creation

BAPI

Business Application Programming Interface – SAP stable API for external access

EDI

Electronic Data Interchange – structured computer-to-computer exchange of business documents

EDIFACT

Electronic Data Interchange For Administration, Commerce and Transport – UN/ECE EDI standard

GS1

Global Standards organization managing GTIN, EAN, GS1-128 barcode standards

GTIN

Global Trade Item Number – globally unique product identifier (successor to UPC/EAN)

IDoc

Intermediate Document – SAP proprietary format for electronic data interchange

ISA

Interchange Control Header – outermost EDI X12 envelope segment

MDN

Message Disposition Notification – AS2 acknowledgment confirming message receipt

PI/PO

SAP Process Integration / Process Orchestration – SAP middleware platform

qRFC

Queued Remote Function Call – guaranteed-order asynchronous RFC communication

RFC

Remote Function Call – SAP remote procedure call mechanism

tRFC

Transactional RFC – exactly-once guaranteed RFC delivery mechanism

UOM

Unit of Measure – standardized unit for quantity expressions (EA, CS, LB, etc.)

VAN

Value-Added Network – third-party EDI mailbox/translation service provider

X12

ANSI X12 – North American EDI standard maintained by ANSI ASC X12 committee

entry.title: Re: SAP DRC Croatia
entry.content: Hello! Did you get the answer to your questions? Thanks!

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone as UI Integration Layer: A Navigation Paradigm Powered by Exposure V2
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Build Work Zone has long been the front door for the SAP landscape, providing a unified entry point to applications across multiple SAP backends systems. Following our learnings over the years in large-scale customer implementation, we have recently updated the UX integration layer paradigm. With this, SAP Build Work Zone sits on-top the individual product shells, rather than replacing them. As a result, end users benefit from having SAP Build Work Zone as the access point to the SAP landscape, while keep gaining the power of the lines of business specific home pages with their unique capabilities. 

Exposure version 2, available as of SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2402, is the technical vehicle that makes this new paradigm possible. In this blog post, I'll explore what this paradigm shift means for customers, how exposure version 2 enables it, what benefits it delivers, and what prerequisites and differences in behavior you should be aware of before adopt.

 

The Traditional Paradigm: One Shell to Run All Applications

In the traditional integration model — supported by exposure version 1 — SAP Build Work Zone acted as a replacement shell. Applications from SAP S/4HANA and other connected systems were launched in-place, meaning they opened embedded directly within the Work Zone shell. Navigation between apps, shell services, and all user interactions happened inside this single, centralized environment.

While this approach offered a unified look and feel, it came with significant trade-offs:

  • Loss of product-specific shell capabilities: Features native to the SAP S/4HANA shell — such as the very popular SAP S/4HANA My Home, built-in support ticket creation, Microsoft Teams integration, and advanced shell plugins — were unavailable when apps ran inside the Work Zone shell.
  • Navigation complexity: Users who needed to work intensively within a single backend system found themselves constrained by a shell that wasn't designed for deep, product-specific workflows.
  • Content integration challenges: Exposure v1 relies on a deprecated content model in S/4HANA which might lead to artificial app splits and inconsistencies with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.

 

The New Paradigm: Work Zone as a UI Integration Layer

Based on extensive user research, SAP discovered that experienced system users prefer to work within their individual product shells. These users don't want their SAP S/4HANA launchpad replaced — they want quick access to cross-system services and then the ability to dive into the product environment they know best.

This insight led to a repositioning of SAP Build Work Zone. Rather than being the single shell for everything, it now serves as a cross-product integration layer focused on providing a starting point that gives users the ability to discover and launch apps across multiple SAP systems and third-party solutions without needing to know which backend hosts them. It offers unified access to central services, e.g. a central search across connected systems so users can find content regardless of where it resides, access to SAP Task Center which delivers a consolidated task inbox that aggregates approvals and workflows from multiple backends, and centralized notifications.

Once a user selects an application, they are directed to the native product shell (e.g., the SAP S/4HANA Fiori launchpad) where the app opens in a new browser tab. All subsequent navigation — app-to-app transitions, shell services, and product-specific features — happens within that product's own environment. Users return to SAP Build Work Zone simply by closing the tab.

This approach creates a clear separation of concerns: Work Zone handles cross-system orchestration, while individual product shells handle product-specific workflows.

 

How Exposure v2 Enables This Paradigm

Exposure v2 was purpose-built to support this integration layer model. Here's how its design choices map directly to the new paradigm:

Design Choice in Exposure v2

How It Supports the Integration Layer Paradigm

Apps launch in the content provider's native shell (new browser tab)

Users land in the product-specific environment with full access to all shell capabilities — no feature loss.

Navigation managed by the content provider shell

App-to-app navigation within SAP S/4HANA stays within S/4HANA, creating cohesive navigation flow

Consistent app model aligned with SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Apps exposed to Work Zone match what users see in the reference library, eliminating artificial splits and confusion.

Semantic App IDs (transaction codes or Fiori IDs)

Apps are easily traceable and identifiable, simplifying administration and troubleshooting across the integration landscape.

Business are directly derived from the new content model

Reflects the modern SAP S/4HANA content architecture, reducing integration errors and maintenance overhead.

Meanwhile, for self-service applications targeting larger or more casual user groups — such as leave requests, time sheet management, or onboarding workflows — administrators can still manually integrate these apps to launch in-place within Work Zone. While the decision for exposure version 1 or 2 is taken on content provider level, this hybrid approach provides additional flexibility: power users get their native product experience, while occasional users get a simple, guided experience without needing to understand backend systems.

 

Benefits of the Integration Layer Approach

Full Access to Product-Specific Capabilities

With apps launching in their native shell, users benefit from all the that SAP S/4HANA (or other products) provide — including advanced shell plugins, built-in support for ticket creation, Microsoft Teams integration, SAP Analytics Cloud embedded apps, and advanced features like Variant Configuration (AVC) or Advanced Available-to-Promise (ATP) components that are not supported with in-place launching.

Clear Navigation Model

The new exposure model helps reduce navigation flaws by placing navigation responsibility squarely with SAP S/4HANA. Rather than layering competing navigation paradigms, the model leverages a pattern users already know: opening a product in a new browser tab and closing it to return. This is a well-established convention used by other vendors who organize navigation to secondary products in the same way, making the experience immediately familiar. Each product is clearly , so there is no ambiguity about which product is in control.

Reduced Maintenance Overhead

Because exposure v2 aligns with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library and uses semantic IDs, administrators spend less time troubleshooting content mismatches, artificial app splits, or broken tile/target mapping combinations.

Flexibility for Diverse User Groups

The paradigm doesn't force an all-or-nothing approach. Exposure v2 only supports the launch in the native product shell, but customers can still integrate cross-system services and casual-user apps manually to have them run in-place, while business-critical transactional apps launch in their native shells. This lets organizations tailor the experience to different user personas.

 

Key Considerations and Constraints

While the integration layer paradigm and exposure v2 offer compelling advantages, there are important constraints customers must evaluate before adoption. First, exposure v2 is only available for SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 or later (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA public Cloud. Older SAP S/4HANA releases, SAP ECC, and SAP Business Suite systems do not support v2. For these systems, the traditional in-place model with exposure v1 remains the only option.

SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Based on Launchpad App Description

Exposure v2 relies on the Launchpad App Descriptor Item (LADI) content structure according to the current SAP S/4HANA best practices and recommendations. Customers whose SAP Fiori launchpad content is still organized using deprecated tile/target mapping structures must complete a migration project to LADIs before their custom content can be federated through v2. Depending on the volume and complexity, especially on S/4HANA 2023 system, this migration can be substantial. The 2025 release introduces significant enhancements that greatly reduce the required effort; see the documentation for details.

Transaction Code Requirement

Only applications with an assigned transaction code are exposed with v2. While almost all standard apps have been assigned transaction codes as of SAP S/4HANA 2025, some legacy components — e.g. apps shipped with UIHR002 (HCM) or UITRV001 (Travel Management) — cannot technically receive a transaction code. Customers with custom SAPUI5, Web Dynpro ABAP, or WCF apps must create transaction codes for these apps as a prerequisite. For SAP S/4HANA 2023, a downport note for missing transaction code assignments to standard apps is under preparation.

User Experience Adjustment

Users accustomed to the in-place model may initially find the new tab-based launching disorienting in the beginning. There is no option for administrators or end users to change this behavior to in-place launching. This is by design but may not suit every organizational preference. SAP recommends enabling the product name display in the shell header so users can immediately identify which system they are working in. Change management and user communication are essential to a smooth transition.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Custom Content

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, only SAP-delivered apps are exposed through v2. Custom apps are not exposed due to the missing transaction code. This can be a significant limitation for customers who have extended their cloud environment, but this topic is planned to be addressed in future SAP S/4HANA public Cloud releases.

Theme Consistency

Because apps launch in the respective product shell, any custom theme used in SAP Build Work Zone should also be transported into all connected SAP systems to ensure a consistent visual experience. Also with the old in-place model (v1), apps relied on the Work Zone theme to be available in their source system, but defining an SAP standard theme as a fallback was often sufficient for a consistent experience. The integration layer model however requires more deliberate theme management across systems.

For further restrictions regarding tile types or considerations, please check the exposure note SAP Note 3345119.

 

Mixed-Mode Considerations

If an organization uses both v1 and v2 within the same Work Zone site — for example, v2 for a current SAP S/4HANA system and v1 for a legacy backend — the result is inconsistent app behavior: some apps open in-place while others launch in a new tab. This should be carefully communicated to users and, where possible, minimized through thoughtful content organization.

 

Who Should Adopt This Paradigm?

The integration layer paradigm powered by exposure v2 is best suited for:

  • Customers on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud or SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 FPS01+ who want to leverage the latest integration architecture.
  • Enterprises with multiple SAP backends where a cross-system entry point with centralized search, tasks, and notifications adds clear value.
  • Customers whose SAP Fiori launchpad content follows the latest LADI-based best practices, or who are willing to invest in migration.

The traditional replacement-shell model with exposure v1 remains appropriate for:

  • Older SAP S/4HANA versions (pre-2023 FPS01), SAP ECC, and SAP Business Suite systems
  • Customers with extensive tile/target mapping content who are not yet ready for LADI migration
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers who need custom apps exposed.
  • Existing satisfied customers: If you’re happy with your current setup and your users are accustomed to the existing navigation paradigm, there’s no need to migrate to the new exposure version — the current version will continue to be supported going forward.

 

Conclusion

The positioning of SAP Build Work Zone is evolving from a central launchpad to a cross-product UX integration layer and exposure version 2 is the mechanism that brings this vision to life. By running apps in their native product shells and focusing Work Zone on app access and cross-system services like search, tasks, and notifications, this paradigm delivers a clearer navigation model, full access to product-specific capabilities, and a more consistent app experience.

However, the transition is not without effort. LADI migration, transaction code prerequisites, theme management across systems, and user change management are all factors that require planning. And for organizations with older backends or specific content requirements, the traditional model remains a valid and supported path.

For customers on supported SAP S/4HANA versions, SAP recommends adopting exposure v2 and the integration layer paradigm as the standard approach to cross-product user experience.

 

Further Reading

  • SAP Note 3345119 – Expose Launchpad Content to SAP BTP - Exposure Version 2
  • Content Exposure Versions Overview on help.sap.com
  • Migrating Technical Catalogs to LADIs: Migration Guide and Best Practices
  • SAP Note 3042853 – Limitations for in-place app launching (exposure v1)

entry.title: Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect
entry.content:
The MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite lets you expose any API as an MCP tool directly from API Management — giving AI agents governed, discoverable access to your systems, whether SAP or non-SAP. Getting the connection working is the easy part. The harder question is: what should your MCP tools actually look like?

What MCP adds on top of your APIs

Enterprise landscapes are rarely uniform. A single business process might touch an OData service in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, a REST API from Salesforce or ServiceNow, a SOAP service in a legacy SAP ECC system, and an RFC function module — each with different conventions and varying levels of documentation. For APIs that lack a proper spec, Integration Suite includes AI-assisted OpenAPI specification generation.

MCP provides a single, consistent interface on top of all of these:

  • Business intent — the tool description tells the agent what the operation means and when to use it
  • Multi-API orchestration — one tool can encapsulate calls across OData, REST, SOAP, and RFC — regardless of vendor
  • Governance — authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging applied consistently to every agent call
  • Broad system coverage — any system reachable via REST, OData, SOAP, RFC, or HTTP

Two ways to design your MCP tools — and why it matters

The endpoint-shaped approach (and its problems)

The natural first step is to expose existing endpoints as MCP tools one-to-one. This works for a demo, but creates problems in practice.

An agent is asked: "Create a new customer for Acme Corp, shipping address in Frankfurt, assigned to sales org DE01."

With endpoint-shaped tools:

Tool: get_business_partner_roles()
Tool: get_company_codes()
Tool: get_sales_organizations()
Tool: create_business_partner(name="Acme Corp", category="2")
Tool: create_business_partner_address(bp_id="...", city="Frankfurt", ...)
Tool: assign_business_partner_role(bp_id="...", role="FLCU01")
Tool: assign_customer_company(bp_id="...", company_code="DE01")
Tool: assign_customer_sales_area(bp_id="...", sales_org="DE01", ...)

Eight tool calls for one user request — each costing tokens and adding latency. The agent must track IDs across calls, reason about partial failures, and reconstruct SAP business logic on every run. If a business rule changes, every prompt that references it needs updating.

The intent-shaped approach

Tool: create_customer(
  name: "Acme Corp",
  city: "Frankfurt",
  country: "DE",
  sales_org: "DE01"
)

One call. The MCP server handles the sequence, enforces business rules, and manages error handling. The agent stays focused on the conversation.

What makes a good intent-shaped tool

  • Named after a business operation, not an API resource (approve_purchase_requisition, not patch_pr_item)
  • Business-meaningful parameters — accept sales_org: "DE01", not a raw ID the agent has to look up
  • Hides the sequence — the agent doesn't need to know that address creation must precede role assignment
  • Encodes business rules — the tool implementation handles logic the agent shouldn't need to reason about: which roles are mandatory, whether the requester has approval authority, what "overdue" means in your context
  • Returns a useful response — a confirmation the agent can relay, not a raw JSON dump

Tool descriptions matter

The tool description is what the agent reads to decide whether and how to use a tool.

Useful:

approve_purchase_requisition — Approves a purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA. Use this when a user wants to approve a pending PR. Checks approval authority, updates the requisition status, adds the approval note, and advances the workflow to the purchasing team.

Not useful:

PATCH endpoint for purchase requisition status update.

How to build this with SAP Integration Suite

Joule / 3rd Party AI Agent
              ↓  MCP protocol
    MCP Gateway (SAP Integration Suite)
       ↓                  ↓                    ↓
  API Artifact       HTTP Endpoint       RFC Destination
  (OData/REST/SOAP)  (any HTTP service)  (S/4HANA / ECC RFCs)
       ↓
  optional: Cloud Integration iFlow
  (multi-system orchestration)
       ↓
  Any Backend (SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, legacy systems, ...)

Three creation paths are supported — choose based on your backend:

SourceWhen to use
Managed API artifact (OData / REST / SOAP)Any API already managed in API Management; add mediation steps inside the artifact if needed
HTTP endpoint + OpenAPI specAny external HTTP service
RFC destinationDirect access to SAP S/4HANA or ECC function modules

For multi-system orchestration, build a Cloud Integration iFlow, expose it as a managed API artifact, and create the MCP server from that.

Once the source is configured: select the operations to expose as tools, write a tool description for each, apply governance policies (authentication, rate limiting, audit logging), and publish to Developer Hub.

For step-by-step setup, see Design APIs and MCP Servers in the SAP Help Portal.

Before you publish: a quick checklist

  • Are your tools intent-shaped? If an agent needs to call three or more tools in sequence for a single user request, consider consolidating them.
  • Are business rules in the server, not the prompt? Sequencing logic and mandatory field rules belong in the MCP server, not in system prompts.
  • Do your tool descriptions explain intent? Write for an agent that understands business language, not for a developer reading API docs.
  • Is every call governed? Authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging should be applied at the API Management layer.
  • Do errors help the agent respond? Return "No business partner found with ID 1234567. Search by name using find_business_partner." — not { error: "PARTNER_NOT_FOUND", code: 404 }.
  • Are write operations clearly named? Tools that post documents, send emails, or trigger workflows should be named and described so the agent and user understand the consequences.

Summary

SAP Integration Suite gives you the complete stack: Cloud Integration for business logic and orchestration, API Management for governance, and the MCP Gateway to surface everything as MCP tools for any compatible AI client.

Build tools around what users want to accomplish — create_customer, approve_purchase_requisition, find_overdue_invoices — not around what endpoints exist. One well-designed tool that handles a complete business operation is more reliable, faster, and cheaper than a chain of granular API calls.

entry.title: Re: SAP RAP
entry.content:
Hallo Juba-JMY,

Ich hatte das selbe Problem mit anderen Funktionsbausteinen im BW Umfeld. Meine Lösung war es hier ein Systemevent(cl_batch_event=>raise)  aufzurufen, auf welches dann ein Hintergrundjob reagiert und ein Programm startet in dem dann die Fubas aufgerufen wurden. Wenn du allerdings auch noch Daten an den FuBa übergeben musst brauchst du noch eine Möglichkeit diese bspw. in einer Z-Tabelle als Queue zwischenzuspeichern oder in den Memory zu schreiben(EXPORT TO MEMORY), sodass der Hintergrundjob diese dann lesen kann. Mir ist bewusst, dass das vmtl. nicht die Lösung des Problems ist, die SAP vorgesehen hat (mir ist aber auch keine andere bekannt). Vielleicht hat jemand der sich mehr mit RAP beschäftigt hat mehr Informationen oder Bestpractises, wie man solche Funktionalitäten die auf Standard FuBa's beruhen umsetzen kann.

 

I encountered the same issue with other function modules in the BW environment. My solution was to trigger a system event (`cl_batch_event=>raise`); a background job would then respond to this event by starting a program that called the function modules. However, if you also need to pass data to the function module, you need a way to stage that data—for instance, by temporarily storing it in a custom (Z) table acting as a queue or by writing it to memory (`EXPORT TO MEMORY`) so the background job can read it. I realize this is likely not the solution SAP intended (though I am not aware of any other). Perhaps someone with more experience in RAP has further information or best practices for implementing functionality that relies on standard function modules in this way.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud dereferencing REF TO DATA into DATA is not working for not released data types
entry.content:
I faced one behavior in BTP ABAP Cloud, which I find weird. Maybe someone can bring more info on the topic.

Imaging we have:

  • software component A - generic tool for something, and can work on generic data type
  • software component B - want to use the tool from A

If I do the tool to accept this data as data ref REF TO data, and this assignment fails in case B sends data ref to not released type (without released API contract).

ASSIGN data_ref->* TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<data>).
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.

Even this one fails, assignment just returns sy-subrc 4.

FIELD-SYMBOLS <data> TYPE data.       
ASSIGN data_ref->* TO <data>.         
IF sy-subrc <> 0.                     
  RETURN.                             
ENDIF.

But when passing just TYPE data, e.g. via CHANGING, the tool can work fine, and can even change this data in dynamic way.

What is difference here? Why reference cannot work? On first look it doesn't bring any dependency, so why first variant via reference cannot work with not released type generically.

I was trying to find info on that WHY, but without success. Found only confirmation of my understanding that de-referencing is not working in software-heroes blog https://software-heroes.com/en/blog/abap-cloud-reusable-components.

entry.title: Re: Custom Validation not working for create incoming inv...
entry.content:
Dear @kent_2804,

The previous analysis was related to displaying a custom validation message for the invoice amount threshold greater than in the above apps. For those scenarios the relevant extension points were confirmed as MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD for the supplier invoice apps and FIN_ACDOC_VALIDATION for Create Incoming Invoices / FB60. Regarding your additional question about Park Incoming Invoice, I would like to clarify the exact action you are referring to because this is a different scenario

In the Park Incoming Invoice / MIR7 the available actions are:

  • Save Parked Document
  • Hold
  • Save as Completed
  • Cancel

For example I reproduced the Save Parked Document scenario in my environment. This action is handled by MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD using:

IF action = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_action_park.

APPEND VALUE #(
messagetype = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_messagetype_error
messageid = 'MRM_BADI_CLOUD'
messagenumber = '001'
messagevariable1 = 'TEST BADI ON MIR7 WHEN CLICK SAVE PARKED DOCUMENT'
) TO messages.

ENDIF.

With this logic the message was displayed in the Message log when clicking Save Parked Document

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Credit Check not Block Deliveries
entry.content:
Why one BP shows delivery values and the other does not is because

Credit exposure categories
In UKM_BP → Credit Segment → Credit Exposure, the system aggregates values by category (Sales Orders, Deliveries, Billing Documents, FI-AR, etc.). If you see delivery values for BP1 but not for BP2, it means the delivery for BP2 was not updated into the credit exposure tables (UKM_TRANSFER / UKM_ITEM). This usually happens if the delivery did not trigger the credit check update (e.g., missing credit segment assignment, incorrect risk class, or the delivery type not flagged for credit relevance).

Order vs. Delivery relevance
You mentioned orders are statistical only (not consuming credit). That is fine. But deliveries must be flagged as credit‑relevant in customizing (OVAD / OVA8 settings for delivery types). If one BP’s delivery is not appearing, the issue is not with the order but with the delivery posting into credit exposure.

Custom transaction calling credit check FM
When you run your custom transaction, it calls the credit check FM (likely UKM_CHECK or SD_CREDIT_CHECK). For BP1, the delivery was already posted into credit exposure, so the FM sees it and blocks correctly. For BP2, the delivery was not posted, so the FM only sees the new delivery you are checking, not the cumulative exposure. That’s why the block only happens on the second delivery.

Check the below areas

  • Delivery type customizing: Ensure the delivery type used for BP2 is marked credit‑relevant in OVA8.
  • Credit segment assignment: Confirm BP2 is assigned to the same credit segment as BP1 in UKM_BP.
  • Update to credit exposure: Check table UKM_ITEM after creating the delivery. If no entry exists for BP2’s delivery, the update routine is missing.
  • Risk category / status: Verify BP2 has a valid risk category and credit control area assignment. If missing, exposure won’t update.
  • User exit / BAdI logic: If you have custom logic in exits like EXIT_SAPFV45K_001 or BAdI UKM_TRANSFER, ensure it doesn’t filter out BP2’s deliveries.

I understand that this question mainly is about how simple you can integrate with your deckling system vs whether we can bend the concept of order combination in the direction to combine only one order.  Which is not really an order combination at all.

Stefan Weisenberger

entry.title: Using Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities - Including Project Stock
entry.content:
Contents

  1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough
  2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)
  3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities
  4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario
  5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile
  6. Key Takeaways
  7. Further Reading
  8. System Example Showing the Process Flow


1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough

When a company plans a significant maintenance activity, an overhaul of a complex machine, a major refurbishment of production equipment, or a large infrastructure repair, the cost and material management requirements quickly exceed what a simple overhead project can provide comfortably.

Key challenges in these scenarios include:

  • Tracking all incurred costs (internal labor, external services, materials) in one structured object across a potentially multi-month timeline.
  • Ring-fencing materials ordered specifically for this activity so they cannot accidentally be consumed by other production or maintenance tasks.
  • At the end of the activity deciding which portion of the costs should be capitalised to the fixed asset and which should be expensed. (As a big overhaul will increase the value of the asset, capitalising at least part of the maintenance costs makes totally sense.)

Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud address all three challenges in a single, coherent setup.

 

2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)

Before focusing on the maintenance use case, it is worth recapping the standard flow because the maintenance scenario builds directly on it.

In a standard investment project, costs for the creation of a new own-used asset are collected on a project and settled to an Asset under Construction (AuC). This settlement to the AuC can be done event-based or period-based. This moves costs off P&L into the balance sheet. Once the asset is ready for use, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Any costs that cannot be capitalised are redirected to a cost center or an overhead project.

  1. Create Investment Project & WBS Structure
    Define the project with an investment profile. The investment profile drives the automatic creation of an Asset under Construction (AuC).
  2. Collect Costs on the Project
    Post actual costs: purchase orders, goods receipts, time confirmations, service entry sheets. All costs land on WBS elements.
  3. Settlement to the AuC
    At period end (or event-based, triggered by every posting to the project), costs are settled from the investment project to the AuC. This removes costs from P&L and places them on the balance sheet.
  4. Final Settlement to the Fixed Asset
    When the asset is complete, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Non-capitalisable costs are distributed to a cost center or overhead project according to the settlement rule.
  5. Asset Activation & Deprecation
    The asset is activated in Asset Accounting and begins regular depreciation according to the assigned depreciation key.

SAP Community Blog Reference
For a detailed walkthrough of the standard settlement process, see the SAP Community blog post: 
Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP at community.sap.com.

 

3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities

Investment projects are not limited to the creation of entirely new assets. They are equally well-suited for larger maintenance activities. For example, a complete overhaul of a turbine, a production line rebuild, or a major refurbishment of a building; where a (substantial) portion of the incurred costs is expected to be capitalised to the existing fixed asset.

Typical Scenario

A company plans an extensive overhaul of a complex machine. The activity is expected to take several weeks, involve multiple vendors and internal teams, and require a large volume of specific spare parts. The total cost is expected to be significant, and management expects that a meaningful portion will extend the useful life or increase the value of the machine - qualifying for capitalisation.

Why an Investment Project Fits

  • Structured cost collection: WBS elements allow costs to be organised by work package/ project phase (e.g., disassembly, repair, reassembly, testing).
  • Flexible settlement: At the end of the overhaul, the settlement rule determines which percentage is posted to the fixed asset (capitalisation) and which goes to a cost center or another cost object (period expense). Both receivers are optional.
  • Audit trail: with event-based settlement all postings are traceable back to their origin - purchase orders, time entries, material consumptions - via the project.

INFO: When to Capitalise Maintenance Costs
Under IFRS and US GAAP, costs that extend the useful life of an asset, increase its capacity, or improve its quality beyond the original standard are capitalisable. Routine repairs that merely maintain the current condition are expensed. The settlement rule in SAP lets you split both in a single settlement run.

Setup Considerations

When the goal is to settle a portion of costs to an existing fixed asset (not create a new one), you have two options:

  • With investment profile — an AuC is created automatically. Costs flow from the project to the AuC during the activity, and finally from the AuC to the existing fixed asset. This approach is preferred when the activity runs across multiple periods and interim balance sheet capitalisation is required.
  • Without investment profile — no AuC is created. Costs accumulate on the project and are settled directly to the existing fixed asset at the end. This simpler variant is covered in Section 5.

 

4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario

For large maintenance activities, materials are often ordered weeks in advance and stored in the warehouse. Without further measures, these materials sit in unrestricted stock and can be issued to any other order or project creating the risk that critical spare parts are consumed elsewhere, delaying the overhaul.

Project stock solves this by ring-fencing materials for a specific WBS element. Any goods receipt for a purchase order referencing that WBS element lands in project stock, not in unrestricted stock. Goods issues from that stock are only possible against the same project.

Availability in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, project stock is available for investment projects and projects with revenue. This is a deliberate design: overhead cost projects do not support project stock in the public cloud edition.

End-to-End Material Flow with Project Stock

  1. Create Purchase Order with Account Assignment to WBS Element
    The buyer creates a purchase order for the required spare parts. The account assignment references the relevant WBS element of the investment project. The account assignment category is set to Q – Project – Make-to-Order.
  2. Goods Receipt into Project Stock
    When the materials arrive, the goods receipt posts them into project stock (a separate stock segment linked exclusively to the WBS element, special stock indicator Q). The materials are visible in inventory management but are reserved for this project only.
  3. Goods Issue from Project Stock to the Project
    When the materials are needed during the overhaul, they are issued from project stock to the WBS element. The cost hits the investment project directly at actual cost.
  4. Settlement of Material Costs Together with Other Project Costs
    Material costs posted to the WBS element are settled along with all other project costs (labor, services) to the AuC, or directly to the fixed asset, or to other receivers like e.g. cost centers, or to a combination of all of these objects. This can be done using the same settlement rule.

5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile

Not every maintenance capitalisation scenario requires the interim step of an Asset under Construction. If the maintenance activity is relatively self-contained and management is comfortable settling directly to the existing fixed asset at completion, an investment project without an investment profile is a simpler and equally valid option.

How It Works

Without an investment profile assigned to the WBS element, no AuC is created automatically. Costs are collected on the project throughout the activity. At the end, the settlement rule points directly to the existing fixed asset (for the capitalisable portion) and to a cost center (for the expensed portion). The settlement is executed once at project close.

INFO: No AuC — But Still an Investment Project
The project must still be created as an investment project (not an overhead cost project) to be eligible for project stock. The absence of an investment profile means no AuC is created, but the project type remains "investment," which is the prerequisite for using project-specific stock in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

When to Use This Variant

  • The maintenance activity is completed within a single fiscal period or a short time frame where interim balance sheet capitalisation is not required.
  • You do not need to report work in progress on the balance sheet during the activity.
  • You want to minimise the number of settlement postings and avoid managing an AuC asset master.

Scenario Comparison at a Glance

Aspect

With Investment Profile (AuC)

Without Investment

Asset under Construction created?

Yes — automatically from investment profile

No — no AuC master record

Interim balance sheet capitalisation?

Yes — costs move to balance sheet at each settlement or even event-based

No — costs remain on the cost object until project close

Settlement target

AuC → final fixed asset at project completion

Directly to the existing fixed asset or other receivers at project close

Project stock eligible?

Yes

Yes — investment project type is still required

Partial capitalisation possible?

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Best suited for

Multi-period projects, large capex activities, new asset creation

Shorter maintenance activities, direct asset improvement, simpler setup

 

6. Key Takeaways

  • Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud are not restricted to new asset creation, they are an effective tool for managing costs of larger maintenance and refurbishment activities.
  • Project stock is available for investment projects (and revenue projects) in the public cloud edition. This makes the investment project the right vehicle when material ring-fencing is required for a major maintenance task.
  • The settlement rule gives you the flexibility to capitalise a portion of maintenance costs to the existing fixed asset while expensing the remainder to a cost center. It is also possible to expense 100% of the maintenance costs. This can be done in a single settlement run.
  • If interim AuC-based balance sheet capitalisation is not required, an investment project without an investment profile simplifies the setup while retaining all cost-collection and project-stock capabilities.

7. Further Reading

  • SAP Community Blog: Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP
  • SAP Help Portal: Scope Item 35F — Project Control: Capital Projects (Best Practices)

 

8. System Example Showing the Process Flow

1. Create Capital Investment Project
when creating the project, you need to use the Project Profile: Investment Project,
otherwise the functionality described in this blog will not be available

The assignment of an investment profile is optional.

  • With an investment profile assigned the system will automatically, with release of the project, create an asset under construction (AuC), you will then periodically or event-based settle the costs from the project to the AuC (move costs out of P&L and capitalise them in the balance sheet).
  • If no investment profile is assigned the system will not create an asset under construction. All the costs posted to the investment project can later be settled to a fixed asset, a cost center, another project,…

2. Capture Planned Costs and/ or Project Budget
If needed, you can upload planned costs or budgets to the investment project.
You can use the app Import Financial Plan Data (F1711) to upload planned costs or project budget.
Planned costs and budgets are differentiated via the plan category. Make sure you choose the category BUDGET01 for the budget upload.

3. Create Demands
If needed, demands can be planned for the investment project. Details are described in scope item 4RF- Project Control – Resourcing and Procurement

4. Purchase Materials into Project Stock

  • create a demand (as described in 3.), or
  • directly create a purchase order (app Manage Purchase Orders) with
    - account assignment category Q – Project – Make-to-Order
    - enter the WBS that should own the stock (item details), you do not need to enter a GL account
    The special stock indicator Q - Project Stock is derived automatically from the WBS account assignment.

    If the purchase order needs an approval, check the tab “Approval Details” to find the assigned approver.
    Approvals can be found via Self Services > My Inbox > Approve Purchase Order

5. Goods Receipt for Project Stock

  • Use the app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843) to post a goods receipt into project stock.
  • Check your project reporting (Project Financial Booklet Booklet – Enterprise Projects) to see the balance sheet posting (material inventory) is assigned/ attributed to the investment project
    The project report shows the inventory account (BS) is account assigned to the project and therefore visible in project reporting. This is only done for project stock.
  • Check your material inventory to see the posted material quantity update in project stock (special stock indicator Q).
    This means, these materials are not freely available but can only be consumed by the assigned WBS element.
    app Stock – Multiple Materials (F1595)

6. Consume Material from Project Stock
Use the app Post Goods Movement (MIGO) to consume the material from project inventory to the investment project.
- Movement Type 221
- Special Stock Indicator Q
enter your WBS element as this project stock can only be consumed for the assigned WBS
the project report shows the consumption:

7. Post Costs to the Investment Project
e.g. activity allocation posting (app Manage Activity Allocation)


8. Maintain Settlement Rules
the settlement rule can be maintained in the project master (app Project Control – Enterprise Projects)

In this example 50% of the costs are capitalised and therefore settled to a fixed asset, the other 50% are settled to a maintenance cost center.
It would also be possible to settle 100% to cost center or other cost objects. There is no need to settle to a fixed asset.

9. Project Settlement
app Run Settlement – Actual

entry.title: Convert both static and dynamic content on S4HANA UI and Fiori screens
entry.content:
Hi,

I have a following customer requirement : customer wants a capability to convert the standard text content on S4HANA UI and Fiori screens from English to Indonesian. In addition to the static field and label text, the requirement is to also convert the dynamic text content to the target language.

I have seen a few community blog posts on the usage of SAP Translation Hub to perform the static message / label conversion for S4HANA, where the translated text is pushed back to the S4HANA system.

How can we  achieve a similar capability for the dynamic text information that is loaded from the HANA database to the SAP UI and Fiori screens ? Can this be achieved through SAP Translation Hub or some other mechanism needs to be thought of ?

Thanks in advance for your response and guidance.
